**Wisconsin**[[a]](#cite_note-13) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest") and [Great Lakes regions](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). It borders [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the west, [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") to the southwest, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the south, [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to the east, [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") to the northeast, and [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") to the north. With a population of about 6 million[[2]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-2) and an area of about 65,500 square miles (170,000 km2), Wisconsin is the [21st-largest state by population](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") and the [23rd-largest by area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"). It has [72 counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Wisconsin "List of counties in Wisconsin"). The state's [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Wisconsin_by_population "List of municipalities in Wisconsin by population") is [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"). Its capital and second-most populous city is [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"); other urban areas include [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") and the [Fox Cities](/wiki/Fox_Cities "Fox Cities").

[Wisconsin's geography](/wiki/Geography_of_Wisconsin "Geography of Wisconsin") is diverse, with dense forests in the north (including [Chequamegon–Nicolet National Forest](/wiki/Chequamegon%E2%80%93Nicolet_National_Forest "Chequamegon–Nicolet National Forest")), rugged unglaciated hills in the western [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area"), and wooded plains, lowlands, and farms stretching from the interior east to Lake Michigan. Wisconsin has the third-longest [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") coastline, after [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and Michigan. At the time of European contact, the area was inhabited by [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples") and [Siouan](/wiki/Siouan "Siouan") nations, and today it is home to [eleven federally recognized tribes](/wiki/List_of_federally_recognized_tribes_by_state#Wisconsin "List of federally recognized tribes by state").[[13]](#cite_note-14) Originally part of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), it was [admitted as a state](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union") in 1848. During the 19th and early 20th centuries, many European settlers entered the state, mostly from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia").[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) Wisconsin remains a center of [German American](/wiki/German_American "German American") and [Scandinavian American](/wiki/Scandinavian_American "Scandinavian American") culture,[[16]](#cite_note-17) particularly in [its cuisine](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Wisconsin "Cuisine of Wisconsin"), with foods such as [bratwurst](/wiki/Bratwurst "Bratwurst") and [kringle](/wiki/Kringle "Kringle").

Wisconsin is one of the nation's leading [dairy producers](/wiki/Dairy_farming "Dairy farming") and is known as "America's Dairyland"; it is particularly famous for [its cheese](/wiki/Wisconsin_cheese "Wisconsin cheese").[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) The state is also famous for [its breweries](/wiki/List_of_breweries_in_Wisconsin "List of breweries in Wisconsin"), with [beer in Milwaukee](/wiki/Beer_in_Milwaukee "Beer in Milwaukee") a longstanding industry. Wisconsin has some of the nation's most permissive [alcohol laws](/wiki/Alcohol_laws_of_Wisconsin "Alcohol laws of Wisconsin") and is known for its [drinking culture](/wiki/Drinking_culture "Drinking culture").[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) Its economy is dominated by manufacturing, healthcare, information technology, and agriculture—specifically dairy, [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberries "Cranberries"), and [ginseng](/wiki/Ginseng "Ginseng").[[21]](#cite_note-22) Tourism is also a major contributor to its economy.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The [gross domestic product](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_GDP "List of states and territories of the United States by GDP") in 2020 was $348 billion.[[23]](#cite_note-24) Wisconsin is home to one UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), comprising [two of the most significant buildings](/wiki/The_20th-Century_Architecture_of_Frank_Lloyd_Wright "The 20th-Century Architecture of Frank Lloyd Wright") designed by Wisconsin-born architect [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright"): his studio at [Taliesin](/wiki/Taliesin_(studio) "Taliesin (studio)") and his [Jacobs I House](/wiki/Herbert_and_Katherine_Jacobs_First_House "Herbert and Katherine Jacobs First House").[[24]](#cite_note-whs-25) Politically, it is considered a [swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") in national and statewide elections; the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") was founded in Wisconsin in 1854.

## Etymology

The word *Wisconsin* originates from the name given to the [Wisconsin River](/wiki/Wisconsin_River "Wisconsin River") by one of the [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages")-speaking Native American groups living in the region at the time of [European colonization](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas").[[25]](#cite_note-26) The French explorer [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette") was the first European to reach the Wisconsin River, arriving in 1673 and calling the river *Meskousing* (likely ᒣᔅᑯᐤᓯᣙ *meskowsin*) in his journal.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Subsequent French writers changed the spelling from *Meskousing* to *Ouisconsin*, and over time this became the name for both the Wisconsin River and the surrounding lands. English speakers [anglicized](/wiki/Anglicized "Anglicized") the spelling from *Ouisconsin* to *Wisconsin* when they began to arrive in large numbers during the early 19th century. The legislature of [Wisconsin Territory](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory") made the current spelling official in 1845.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The [Algonquian](/wiki/Algonquian_languages "Algonquian languages") word for Wisconsin and its original meaning have both grown obscure. While interpretations vary, most implicate the river and the red sandstone that lines its banks. One leading theory holds that the name originated from the [Miami](/wiki/Miami-Illinois_language "Miami-Illinois language") word *Meskonsing*, meaning 'it lies red', a reference to the setting of the Wisconsin River as it flows through the reddish sandstone of the [Wisconsin Dells](/wiki/Dells_of_the_Wisconsin_River "Dells of the Wisconsin River").[[28]](#cite_note-29) Other theories include claims that the name originated from one of a variety of [Ojibwa](/wiki/Ojibwa "Ojibwa") words meaning 'red stone place', 'where the waters gather', or 'great rock'.[[29]](#cite_note-30) More recently, [University of Wisconsin-Green Bay](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin-Green_Bay "University of Wisconsin-Green Bay") lecturer and [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") elder Napos Turney suggested the origin of the name may be the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee_language "Menominee language") word *Wēskōhsaeh*, meaning "good place".[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

## History

Main article: [History of Wisconsin](/wiki/History_of_Wisconsin "History of Wisconsin")

### Early history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Ancient_Aztalan_Village_DSC_0_121.jpg/250px-Ancient_Aztalan_Village_DSC_0_121.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ancient_Aztalan_Village_DSC_0_121.jpg)

[Aztalan State Park](/wiki/Aztalan_State_Park "Aztalan State Park") marks the site of an ancient [Mississippian culture](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture") settlement that flourished during the 10th to 13th centuries.

Wisconsin has been home to a wide variety of cultures over the past 14,000 years. The first people arrived around 10,000 BCE during the [Wisconsin Glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Glaciation "Wisconsin Glaciation"). These early inhabitants, called [Paleo-Indians](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians"), hunted now-extinct [ice age animals](/wiki/Pleistocene#Fauna "Pleistocene") such as the [Boaz mastodon](/wiki/Boaz_mastodon "Boaz mastodon"), a prehistoric [mastodon](/wiki/Mastodon "Mastodon") skeleton unearthed along with spear points in southwest Wisconsin.[[32]](#cite_note-33) After the ice age ended around 8000 BCE, people in the subsequent [Archaic period](/wiki/Archaic_period_in_the_Americas "Archaic period in the Americas") lived by hunting, fishing, and gathering food from wild plants. Agricultural societies emerged gradually over the [Woodland period](/wiki/Woodland_period "Woodland period") between 1000 BCE to 1000 CE. Toward the end of this period, Wisconsin was the heartland of the "[Effigy Mound](/wiki/Effigy_Mound "Effigy Mound") culture", which built thousands of animal-shaped mounds across the landscape.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Later, between 1000 and 1500 CE, the [Mississippian](/wiki/Mississippian_culture "Mississippian culture") and [Oneota](/wiki/Oneota "Oneota") cultures built substantial settlements including the fortified village at [Aztalan](/wiki/Aztalan "Aztalan") in southeast Wisconsin.[[34]](#cite_note-35) The Oneota may be the ancestors of the modern [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa_people "Iowa people") and [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") nations who shared the Wisconsin region with the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") at the time of European contact.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Other Native American groups living in Wisconsin when Europeans first settled included the [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe"), [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"), [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), [Kickapoo](/wiki/Kickapoo_people "Kickapoo people"), and [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), who migrated to Wisconsin from the east between 1500 and 1700.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### European settlements

Main articles: [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), [Canada (New France)](/wiki/Canada_(New_France) "Canada (New France)"), [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), [Treaty of Paris (1763)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)"), [Province of Quebec (1763–1791)](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)"), and [Indian Reserve (1763)](/wiki/Indian_Reserve_(1763) "Indian Reserve (1763)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Jean_Nicolet.jpg/330px-Jean_Nicolet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean_Nicolet.jpg)

[Jean Nicolet](/wiki/Jean_Nicolet "Jean Nicolet"), depicted in a 1910 painting by Frank Rohrbeck, was probably the first European to explore Wisconsin. The mural is located in the [Brown County Courthouse](/wiki/Brown_County_Courthouse_(Wisconsin) "Brown County Courthouse (Wisconsin)") in Green Bay.

The first European to visit what became Wisconsin was probably the French explorer [Jean Nicolet](/wiki/Jean_Nicolet "Jean Nicolet"). He canoed west from [Georgian Bay](/wiki/Georgian_Bay "Georgian Bay") through the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") in 1634, and it is traditionally assumed that he came ashore near [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") at [Red Banks](/wiki/Red_Banks,_Brown_County,_Wisconsin "Red Banks, Brown County, Wisconsin").[[37]](#cite_note-38) [Pierre Radisson](/wiki/Pierre_Radisson "Pierre Radisson") and [Médard des Groseilliers](/wiki/M%C3%A9dard_des_Groseilliers "Médard des Groseilliers") visited Green Bay again in 1654–1666 and [Chequamegon Bay](/wiki/Chequamegon_Bay "Chequamegon Bay") in 1659–1660, where they traded for fur with local Native Americans.[[38]](#cite_note-39) In 1673, Jacques Marquette and [Louis Jolliet](/wiki/Louis_Jolliet "Louis Jolliet") became the first to record a journey on the [Fox-Wisconsin Waterway](/wiki/Fox-Wisconsin_Waterway "Fox-Wisconsin Waterway") all the way to the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") near [Prairie du Chien](/wiki/Prairie_du_Chien,_Wisconsin "Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin").[[39]](#cite_note-40) [Frenchmen](/wiki/Frenchmen "Frenchmen") like [Nicholas Perrot](/wiki/Nicholas_Perrot "Nicholas Perrot") continued to ply the [fur trade](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") across Wisconsin through the 17th and 18th centuries, but the French made no permanent settlements in Wisconsin before [Great Britain](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") won control of the region following the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War") in 1763. Even so, French traders continued to work in the region after the war, and some, beginning with [Charles de Langlade](/wiki/Charles_de_Langlade "Charles de Langlade") in 1764, settled in Wisconsin permanently, rather than returning to British-controlled Canada.[[40]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_Historical_Society-41)

The British gradually took over Wisconsin during the French and Indian War, taking control of Green Bay in 1761 and gaining control of all of Wisconsin in 1763. Like the French, the British were interested in little but the fur trade. One notable event in the fur trading industry in Wisconsin occurred in 1791, when two free African Americans set up a fur trading post among the Menominee at present-day [Marinette](/wiki/Marinette,_Wisconsin "Marinette, Wisconsin"). The first permanent settlers, mostly [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian"), some Anglo-[New Englanders](/wiki/New_England "New England") and a few African American freedmen, arrived in Wisconsin while it was under British control. Charles de Langlade is generally recognized as the first settler, establishing a trading post at Green Bay in 1745, and moving there permanently in 1764.[[40]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_Historical_Society-41) Settlement began at Prairie du Chien around 1781. The French residents at the trading post in what is now Green Bay, referred to the town as "La Baye". However, British fur traders referred to it as "Green Bay", because the water and the shore assumed green tints in early spring. The old French title was gradually dropped, and the British name of "Green Bay" eventually stuck. The region coming under British rule had virtually no adverse effect on the French residents as the British needed the cooperation of the French fur traders and the French fur traders needed the goodwill of the British. During the French occupation of the region licenses for fur trading had been issued scarcely and only to select groups of traders, whereas the British, in an effort to make as much money as possible from the region, issued licenses for fur trading freely, both to British and to French residents. The fur trade in what is now Wisconsin reached its height under British rule, and the first self-sustaining farms in the state were established as well. From 1763 to 1780, Green Bay was a prosperous community which produced its own foodstuff, built graceful cottages and held dances and festivities.[[41]](#cite_note-42)

Joseph Roi built the [Tank Cottage](/wiki/Tank_Cottage "Tank Cottage") in [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") in 1776. Located in [Heritage Hill State Historical Park](/wiki/Heritage_Hill_State_Historical_Park "Heritage Hill State Historical Park"), it is the [oldest standing building](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_buildings_in_Wisconsin "List of the oldest buildings in Wisconsin") from Wisconsin's early years and is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[42]](#cite_note-NRHP-43)

### U.S. territory

Main articles: [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance"), [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"), [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory"), [Illinois Territory](/wiki/Illinois_Territory "Illinois Territory"), [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory"), [Organic act § List of organic acts](/wiki/Organic_act#List_of_organic_acts "Organic act"), and [Wisconsin Territory](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Vertefeuille.JPG/250px-Vertefeuille.JPG)](/wiki/File:Vertefeuille.JPG)

The [Francois Vertefeuille House](/wiki/Francois_Vertefeuille_House "Francois Vertefeuille House") in [Prairie du Chien](/wiki/Prairie_du_Chien,_Wisconsin "Prairie du Chien, Wisconsin") was built in the 1810s by fur traders.

Wisconsin became a territorial possession of the United States in 1783 after the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). In 1787, it became part of the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory"). As territorial boundaries subsequently developed, it was then part of [Indiana Territory](/wiki/Indiana_Territory "Indiana Territory") from 1800 to 1809, [Illinois Territory](/wiki/Illinois_Territory "Illinois Territory") from 1809 to 1818, and [Michigan Territory](/wiki/Michigan_Territory "Michigan Territory") from 1818 to 1836. However, the British remained in control until after the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the outcome of which finally established an American presence in the area.[[43]](#cite_note-44) Under American control, the economy of the territory shifted from fur trading to lead mining. The prospect of easy mineral wealth drew immigrants from throughout the U.S. and Europe to the lead deposits at [Mineral Point](/wiki/Mineral_Point,_Wisconsin "Mineral Point, Wisconsin"), [Dodgeville](/wiki/Dodgeville,_Wisconsin "Dodgeville, Wisconsin"), and nearby areas. Some miners found shelter in the holes they had dug, and earned the nickname "badgers", leading to Wisconsin's identity as the "Badger State".[[44]](#cite_note-45) The sudden influx of white miners prompted tension with the local Native American population. The [Winnebago War](/wiki/Winnebago_War "Winnebago War") of 1827 and the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War") of 1832 culminated in the forced [removal of Native Americans](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") from most parts of the state.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Following these conflicts, [Wisconsin Territory](/wiki/Wisconsin_Territory "Wisconsin Territory") was created by an act of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") on April 20, 1836. By fall of that year, the best prairie groves of the counties surrounding what is now Milwaukee were occupied by farmers from the [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") states.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Statehood

Main articles: [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union") and [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/3c_Wisconsin_Statehood_Centennial%2C_1948_issue.jpg/250px-3c_Wisconsin_Statehood_Centennial%2C_1948_issue.jpg)](/wiki/File:3c_Wisconsin_Statehood_Centennial,_1948_issue.jpg)

On May 29, 1948, the U.S. Post Office issued a [commemorative stamp](/wiki/Commemorative_stamp "Commemorative stamp") celebrating the 100th anniversary of Wisconsin statehood, featuring the state capitol building and map of Wisconsin.

The Great Lakes, via the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal"), facilitated the travel of both [Yankee](/wiki/Yankee "Yankee") settlers and European immigrants to Wisconsin Territory. Yankees from New England and [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") seized a dominant position in law and politics, enacting policies that marginalized the region's earlier Native American and French-Canadian residents.[[47]](#cite_note-48) Yankees also speculated in real estate, platted towns such as Racine, Beloit, Burlington, and Janesville, and established schools, civic institutions, and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") churches.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) At the same time, many [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), Irish, [Norwegians](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians"), and other immigrants also settled in towns and farms across the territory, establishing [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") institutions.

The growing population allowed Wisconsin to gain statehood on May 29, 1848, as the 30th state. Between 1840 and 1850, Wisconsin's non-Indian population had swollen from 31,000 to 305,000. More than a third of residents (110,500) were foreign born, including 38,000 Germans, 28,000 British immigrants from England, Scotland, and Wales, and 21,000 Irish. Another third (103,000) were Yankees from New England and western New York state. Only about 63,000 residents in 1850 had been born in Wisconsin.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

[Nelson Dewey](/wiki/Nelson_Dewey "Nelson Dewey"), the first [governor of Wisconsin](/wiki/Governor_of_Wisconsin "Governor of Wisconsin"), was a [Democrat](/wiki/History_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "History of the Democratic Party (United States)"). Dewey oversaw the transition from the territorial to the new state government.[[52]](#cite_note-1960bio-53) He encouraged the development of the state's infrastructure, particularly the construction of new roads, railroads, canals, and harbors, as well as the improvement of the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_River_(Illinois_River_tributary) "Fox River (Illinois River tributary)") and [Wisconsin Rivers](/wiki/Wisconsin_River "Wisconsin River").[[52]](#cite_note-1960bio-53) During his administration, the [State Board of Public Works](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_Board_of_Public_Works&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wisconsin Board of Public Works (page does not exist)") was organized.[[52]](#cite_note-1960bio-53) Dewey, an [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionist "Abolitionist"), was the first of many Wisconsin governors to advocate against the spread of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") into new states and territories.[[52]](#cite_note-1960bio-53)

Further information: [Pioneer Women in Wisconsin](/wiki/Pioneer_Women_in_Wisconsin "Pioneer Women in Wisconsin")

### Civil War era and economic growth

Main article: [Wisconsin in the American Civil War](/wiki/Wisconsin_in_the_American_Civil_War "Wisconsin in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg/250px-LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:LittleWhiteSchoolhouse.jpg)

The [Little White Schoolhouse](/wiki/Little_White_Schoolhouse "Little White Schoolhouse") in [Ripon](/wiki/Ripon,_Wisconsin "Ripon, Wisconsin") held the nation's first meeting of the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").

Politics in early Wisconsin were defined by the greater national debate over slavery. A free state from its foundation, Wisconsin became a center of northern [abolitionism](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States"). The debate became especially intense in 1854 after [Joshua Glover](/wiki/Joshua_Glover "Joshua Glover"), a runaway slave from [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), was captured in [Racine](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin"). Glover was taken into custody under the Federal [Fugitive Slave Law](/wiki/Fugitive_Slave_Act_of_1850 "Fugitive Slave Act of 1850"), but a mob of abolitionists stormed the prison where Glover was held and helped him escape to Canada. In a trial stemming from the incident, the [Wisconsin Supreme Court](/wiki/Wisconsin_Supreme_Court "Wisconsin Supreme Court") ultimately declared the Fugitive Slave Law unconstitutional.[[53]](#cite_note-54) The [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), founded on March 20, 1854, by anti-slavery expansion activists in [Ripon, Wisconsin](/wiki/Ripon,_Wisconsin "Ripon, Wisconsin"), grew to dominate state politics in the aftermath of these events.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), around 91,000 troops from Wisconsin fought for the [Union](/wiki/Union_(American_Civil_War) "Union (American Civil War)").[[55]](#cite_note-56) A number of Wisconsin regiments were distinguished, including three that served in the celebrated "[Iron Brigade](/wiki/Iron_Brigade "Iron Brigade")"— the [2nd Wisconsin](/wiki/2nd_Wisconsin_Infantry_Regiment "2nd Wisconsin Infantry Regiment"), [6th Wisconsin](/wiki/6th_Wisconsin_Infantry_Regiment "6th Wisconsin Infantry Regiment"), and [7th Wisconsin](/wiki/7th_Wisconsin_Infantry_Regiment "7th Wisconsin Infantry Regiment"). The [8th Wisconsin](/wiki/8th_Wisconsin_Infantry_Regiment "8th Wisconsin Infantry Regiment"), another hard-fighting regiment, was often accompanied into battle by its mascot, [Old Abe](/wiki/Old_Abe "Old Abe"), a bald eagle.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Chase_Stone_Barn_-_Green_Grass.jpg/250px-Chase_Stone_Barn_-_Green_Grass.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chase_Stone_Barn_-_Green_Grass.jpg)

The [Daniel E. Krause Stone Barn](/wiki/Daniel_E._Krause_Stone_Barn "Daniel E. Krause Stone Barn") in [Chase](/wiki/Chase,_Wisconsin "Chase, Wisconsin") was built in 1903, as dairy farming spread across the state.

Wisconsin's economy also diversified during the early years of statehood. While lead mining diminished, agriculture became a principal occupation in the southern half of the state. Railroads were built across the state to help transport grains to market, and industries like [J.I. Case & Company](/wiki/Case_Corporation "Case Corporation") in Racine were founded to build agricultural equipment. Wisconsin briefly became one of the nation's leading producers of wheat during the 1860s.[[56]](#cite_note-57) Meanwhile, the lumber industry dominated in the heavily forested northern sections of Wisconsin, and sawmills sprang up in cities like [La Crosse](/wiki/La_Crosse "La Crosse"), [Eau Claire](/wiki/Eau_Claire,_Wisconsin "Eau Claire, Wisconsin"), and [Wausau](/wiki/Wausau,_Wisconsin "Wausau, Wisconsin"). These economic activities had dire environmental consequences. By the close of the 19th century, intensive agriculture had devastated soil fertility, and lumbering had deforested most of the state.[[57]](#cite_note-58) These conditions forced both wheat agriculture and the lumber industry into a precipitous decline.

Beginning in the 1890s, farmers in Wisconsin shifted from wheat to dairy production to make more sustainable and profitable use of their land. Many immigrants carried cheese-making traditions that, combined with the state's suitable geography and dairy research led by [Stephen Babcock](/wiki/Stephen_Babcock "Stephen Babcock") at the [University of Wisconsin](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin "University of Wisconsin"), helped the state build a reputation as "America's Dairyland".[[58]](#cite_note-59) Meanwhile, conservationists including [Aldo Leopold](/wiki/Aldo_Leopold "Aldo Leopold") helped re-establish the state's forests during the early 20th century,[[59]](#cite_note-60) paving the way for a more renewable lumber and [paper milling](/wiki/Paper_mill "Paper mill") industry as well as promoting recreational tourism in the northern woodlands. Manufacturing also boomed in Wisconsin during the early 20th century, driven by an immense immigrant workforce arriving from Europe. Industries in cities like Milwaukee ranged from brewing and food processing to heavy machine production and tool-making, leading Wisconsin to rank 8th among U.S. states in total product value by 1910.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Progressive era and World Wars

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Suffragists_campaigning_in_Wisconsin%2C_June_7%2C_1916.jpg/250px-Suffragists_campaigning_in_Wisconsin%2C_June_7%2C_1916.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suffragists_campaigning_in_Wisconsin,_June_7,_1916.jpg)

[Suffragists](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_Wisconsin "Women's suffrage in Wisconsin") campaigning, 1916. Wisconsin was among the earliest states to ratify the [Nineteenth Amendment](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution").[[61]](#cite_note-62)

The early 20th century was notable for the emergence of [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States") politics championed by [Robert M. La Follette](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette "Robert M. La Follette"), who served as governor from 1901 to 1906 and U.S. senator from 1906 to 1925. Between 1901 and 1914, Progressive Republicans in Wisconsin created the nation's first comprehensive statewide [primary election](/wiki/Partisan_primary "Partisan primary") system,[[62]](#cite_note-63) the first effective [workplace injury compensation](/wiki/Workers%27_compensation "Workers' compensation") law,[[63]](#cite_note-64) and the first state [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax"),[[64]](#cite_note-65) making taxation proportional to actual earnings.

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), due to the neutrality of Wisconsin and many of its [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_of_Wisconsin "Republican Party of Wisconsin"), [Progressives](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States"), and [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States"), including [Germans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") and [Scandinavians](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans") who were a significant proportion of the state's population, Wisconsin began to be accused of being the "Traitor State" by many "hyper patriots."[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69)

As the war raged on in Europe, La Follette led the antiwar movement in Wisconsin and steered a group of progressive senators in blocking a bill by President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") that would have armed merchant ships with guns. Many Wisconsin politicians, such as [Emanuel L. Philipp](/wiki/Emanuel_L._Philipp "Emanuel L. Philipp") and [Irvine Lenroot](/wiki/Irvine_Lenroot "Irvine Lenroot"), were accused of having divided loyalties.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Even with outspoken opponents to the war, at the onset of the war, many Wisconsinites would abandon neutrality. Businesses, labor and farms all enjoyed prosperity from the war. With over 118,000 going into military service, Wisconsin was the first state to report for the national drafts conducted by the [U.S. military](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces").[[70]](#cite_note-71) As governor, Philipp was successful in combating [anti-German hysteria](/wiki/Anti-German_sentiment "Anti-German sentiment") in the state.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

The progressive [Wisconsin Idea](/wiki/Wisconsin_Idea "Wisconsin Idea") also promoted the statewide expansion of the University of Wisconsin through the [UW–Extension](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Extension "University of Wisconsin–Extension") system at this time.[[72]](#cite_note-73) In 1932, UW economics professors [John R. Commons](/wiki/John_R._Commons "John R. Commons") and Harold Groves helped Wisconsin create the United States' first [unemployment compensation](/wiki/Unemployment_compensation "Unemployment compensation") program.[[73]](#cite_note-74) Other [Wisconsin Idea](/wiki/Wisconsin_Idea "Wisconsin Idea") scholars at the university generated the plan that became the New Deal's [Social Security Act](/wiki/Social_Security_Act "Social Security Act") of 1935, with Wisconsin expert [Arthur J. Altmeyer](/wiki/Arthur_J._Altmeyer "Arthur J. Altmeyer") playing a key role.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c1/LaFollette-Philip-Announces-380428.jpg/250px-LaFollette-Philip-Announces-380428.jpg)](/wiki/File:LaFollette-Philip-Announces-380428.jpg)

Governor [Philip La Follette](/wiki/Philip_La_Follette "Philip La Follette") announces formation of the [National Progressives of America](/wiki/National_Progressives_of_America "National Progressives of America"), 1938

After La Follette died, his sons [Philip](/wiki/Philip_La_Follette "Philip La Follette") and [Robert Jr.](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette_Jr. "Robert M. La Follette Jr.") took over the Wisconsin Republican Party, later founding the [Wisconsin Progressive Party](/wiki/Wisconsin_Progressive_Party "Wisconsin Progressive Party") as a successor to [their father's Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1924%E2%80%931927) "Progressive Party (United States, 1924–1927)"). It gained momentum in the mid-1930s with support from President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") and progressive Democrats, winning offices statewide and in Congress. The party declined after Philip, facing scandal and accusations of authoritarianism, [lost reelection in 1938](/wiki/1938_Wisconsin_gubernatorial_election "1938 Wisconsin gubernatorial election") to [Julius P. Heil](/wiki/Julius_P._Heil "Julius P. Heil") and left politics to serve in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The [National Progressives of America](/wiki/National_Progressives_of_America "National Progressives of America"), an organization Philip had hoped would precede a national realignment, then faltered, and the Wisconsin Progressives eventually voted to dissolve.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### Mid-20th century to present

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion** with: Needs additional Cold War-era and early 21st century history.. You can help by [making an edit request](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Talk%3AWisconsin&preload=Template%3ASubmit+an+edit+request%2Fpreload&action=edit&section=new&editintro=Template%3AEdit+fully-protected%2Feditintro&preloadtitle=Protected+edit+request+on+27+March+2026&preloadparams%5B%5D=edit+fully-protected&preloadparams%5B%5D=Wisconsin)[adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin&action=edit&section=). *(September 2025)* |

In World War II's immediate aftermath, Wisconsinites were divided over issues such as the creation of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), support for the European recovery, and the growth of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union")'s power. But when [Europe divided into Communist and capitalist camps](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain"), and with the Progressive Party's collapse, [Robert La Follette Jr.](/wiki/Robert_M._La_Follette_Jr. "Robert M. La Follette Jr.") rejoined the Republican Party of Wisconsin to run in the 1946 Senate election. He narrowly lost to [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy") in the [Republican primary](/wiki/1946_United_States_Senate_election_in_Wisconsin "1946 United States Senate election in Wisconsin"). After the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") of 1949, public opinion began to continue move toward support for the protection of democracy and capitalism against Communist expansion.[[77]](#cite_note-:3-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Swing_bridge%2C_Superior%2C_Wisconsin_%28cut%29.jpg/250px-Swing_bridge%2C_Superior%2C_Wisconsin_%28cut%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Swing_bridge,_Superior,_Wisconsin_(cut).jpg)

In the 20th century, [Superior](/wiki/Superior,_Wisconsin "Superior, Wisconsin") was a major hub for shipping iron ore across the Great Lakes via [lake freighters](/wiki/Lake_freighter "Lake freighter").

Wisconsin took part in several political extremes in the mid- to late 20th century, ranging from the [anti-communist](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") crusades of Senator McCarthy in the 1950s, to the founding of [Earth Day](/wiki/Earth_Day "Earth Day") by environmental advocate [Gaylord Nelson](/wiki/Gaylord_Nelson "Gaylord Nelson") in 1970. During radical protests against the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") at UW-Madison, an attack by anarchists culminated in the [Sterling Hall bombing](/wiki/Sterling_Hall_bombing "Sterling Hall bombing") in August 1970. The state undertook [welfare reform](/wiki/Welfare_reform "Welfare reform") under Republican Governor [Tommy Thompson](/wiki/Tommy_Thompson "Tommy Thompson") during the 1990s.[[78]](#cite_note-79) Its economy also underwent further transformations toward the end of the 20th century, as heavy industry and manufacturing declined in favor of a [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy") based on medicine, education, agribusiness, and tourism.

In 2011, Wisconsin became the focus of some controversy when newly elected governor [Scott Walker](/wiki/Scott_Walker_(politician) "Scott Walker (politician)") proposed and then passed and enacted [2011 Wisconsin Act 10](/wiki/2011_Wisconsin_Act_10 "2011 Wisconsin Act 10"), which made large changes in the areas of collective bargaining, compensation, retirement, health insurance, and sick leave of public sector employees, among other changes.[[79]](#cite_note-80) A [series of major protests](/wiki/2011_Wisconsin_protests "2011 Wisconsin protests") by union supporters took place that year in protest to the changes, and Walker survived [a recall election held the next year in 2012](/wiki/Wisconsin_gubernatorial_recall_election "Wisconsin gubernatorial recall election"), becoming the first governor in U.S. history to do so.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Also in 2012, Congressman [Paul Ryan](/wiki/Paul_Ryan "Paul Ryan") became the first Wisconsinite to appear on a major party ticket, as Republican nominee [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney")'s running mate in the [2012 United States presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"). Ryan was elected [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") in 2015.

Since 2023, [five University of Wisconsin branch campuses have closed](/wiki/Closures_of_the_University_of_Wisconsin_branch_campuses "Closures of the University of Wisconsin branch campuses"), one has gone entirely online, and one has stopped using several of its buildings. Many have expressed concern about the Wisconsin Idea's future since these closures.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Wisconsin](/wiki/Geography_of_Wisconsin "Geography of Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Wisconsin_geographic_provinces.svg/250px-Wisconsin_geographic_provinces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_geographic_provinces.svg)

Wisconsin geographic regions

Wisconsin is in the [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and is part of both the [Great Lakes region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") and the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"). The state has a total area of 65,496 square miles (169,630 km2). Wisconsin is bordered by [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") and [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") to the north; by [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") to the east; by [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") to the south; and by [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") to the southwest and [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") to the northwest. A border dispute with Michigan was settled by two cases, both [Wisconsin v. Michigan](/wiki/Wisconsin_v._Michigan "Wisconsin v. Michigan"), in 1934 and 1935. The state's boundaries include the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") and [St. Croix River](/wiki/St._Croix_River_(Wisconsin%E2%80%93Minnesota) "St. Croix River (Wisconsin–Minnesota)") in the west, and the [Menominee River](/wiki/Menominee_River "Menominee River") and [Montreal River](/wiki/Montreal_River_(Wisconsin%E2%80%93Michigan) "Montreal River (Wisconsin–Michigan)") in the northeast.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Lying between the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and the Mississippi River, Wisconsin has a wide variety of geographical features. The state is divided into five distinct regions. In the north, the [Lake Superior Lowland](/wiki/Lake_Superior_Lowland "Lake Superior Lowland") occupies a belt of land along Lake Superior. Just to the south, the [Northern Highland](/wiki/Northern_Highland "Northern Highland") has massive mixed hardwood and coniferous forests including the 1,500,000-acre (610,000-hectare) [Chequamegon–Nicolet National Forest](/wiki/Chequamegon%E2%80%93Nicolet_National_Forest "Chequamegon–Nicolet National Forest"), as well as thousands of glacial lakes, and the state's highest point, [Timms Hill](/wiki/Timms_Hill "Timms Hill"). In the middle of the state, the [Central Plain](/wiki/Central_Plain_(Wisconsin) "Central Plain (Wisconsin)") has some unique [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") formations like the [Dells of the Wisconsin River](/wiki/Dells_of_the_Wisconsin_River "Dells of the Wisconsin River") in addition to rich farmland. The [Eastern Ridges and Lowlands](/wiki/Eastern_Ridges_and_Lowlands "Eastern Ridges and Lowlands") region in the southeast is home to many of Wisconsin's largest cities. The ridges include the [Niagara Escarpment](/wiki/Niagara_Escarpment "Niagara Escarpment") that stretches from New York, the [Black River Escarpment](/wiki/Black_River_Escarpment_(Wisconsin) "Black River Escarpment (Wisconsin)") and the [Magnesian Escarpment](/wiki/Magnesian_Escarpment "Magnesian Escarpment").[[85]](#cite_note-Martin1965-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87) In the southwest, the [Western Upland](/wiki/Western_Upland "Western Upland") is a rugged landscape with a mix of forest and farmland, including many bluffs on the Mississippi River, and the [Baraboo Range](/wiki/Baraboo_Range "Baraboo Range"). This region is part of the [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area"), which also includes parts of Iowa, Illinois, and Minnesota. Overall, 46% of Wisconsin's land area is covered by forest.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/TypicalDriftless.jpg/250px-TypicalDriftless.jpg)](/wiki/File:TypicalDriftless.jpg)

The [Driftless Area](/wiki/Driftless_Area "Driftless Area") is characterized by bluffs carved in [sedimentary](/wiki/Sedimentary "Sedimentary") rock by water from melting [Ice age](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age") glaciers.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Big_waves_on_Devils_Island_shoreline_%28e99f5cd1-548c-439d-962d-0c03204c2bf8%29.jpg/250px-Big_waves_on_Devils_Island_shoreline_%28e99f5cd1-548c-439d-962d-0c03204c2bf8%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Big_waves_on_Devils_Island_shoreline_(e99f5cd1-548c-439d-962d-0c03204c2bf8).jpg)

[Sea caves](/wiki/Sea_caves "Sea caves") are located on the shorelines of the [Apostle Islands](/wiki/Apostle_Islands "Apostle Islands") in [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior").

Wisconsin has geologic formations and deposits that vary in age from over three billion years to several thousand years, with most rocks being millions of years old.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The oldest geologic formations were created over 600 million years ago during the [Precambrian](/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian"), the majority below the glacial deposits. Much of the Baraboo Range consists of [Baraboo Quartzite](/wiki/Baraboo_Quartzite "Baraboo Quartzite") and other Precambrian [metamorphic rock](/wiki/Metamorphic_rock "Metamorphic rock").[[88]](#cite_note-Hanson-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90) This area was not covered by [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") during the most recent ice age, the [Wisconsin Glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Glaciation "Wisconsin Glaciation"). [Langlade County](/wiki/Langlade_County "Langlade County") has a soil rarely found outside the county called [Antigo silt loam](/wiki/Antigo_(soil) "Antigo (soil)").[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The state has more than 12,000 named rivers and streams, totaling 84,000 miles (135,000 km) in length.[[91]](#cite_note-92) It has over 15,000 named lakes, totaling about 1 million acres (4,000 km2). [Lake Winnebago](/wiki/Lake_Winnebago "Lake Winnebago") is the largest inland lake, with over 137,700 acres (557 km2), and 88 miles (142 km) of shoreline. Along the two Great Lakes, Wisconsin has over 800 miles (1,300 km) of shoreline (over 500 miles (800 km), not counting minor islands and indentations).[[92]](#cite_note-Martin_(1916)_p._21-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) Many of the [named islands in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Wisconsin "List of islands of Wisconsin") are in the Great Lakes; many surround the [Door Peninsula](/wiki/Door_Peninsula "Door Peninsula") in Lake Michigan or are part of the [Apostle Islands](/wiki/Apostle_Islands "Apostle Islands") in Lake Superior.[[94]](#cite_note-95) The Mississippi River and inland lakes and rivers contain the rest of Wisconsin's islands.

Areas under the protection of the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") include the [Apostle Islands National Lakeshore](/wiki/Apostle_Islands_National_Lakeshore "Apostle Islands National Lakeshore"), [Ice Age National Scenic Trail](/wiki/Ice_Age_Trail "Ice Age Trail"), and portions of the [Saint Croix National Scenic Riverway](/wiki/Saint_Croix_National_Scenic_Riverway "Saint Croix National Scenic Riverway") and [North Country National Scenic Trail](/wiki/North_Country_Trail "North Country Trail").[[95]](#cite_note-96) There are an additional 18 [National Natural Landmarks](/wiki/List_of_National_Natural_Landmarks_in_Wisconsin "List of National Natural Landmarks in Wisconsin") in the state that include dune and swales, swamps, bogs, and old-growth forests. Wisconsin has [50 state park units](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_state_parks "List of Wisconsin state parks"), covering more than 60,570 acres (245.1 km2) in state parks and state recreation areas maintained by the [Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Natural_Resources "Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources"). The Division of Forestry manages a further 471,329 acres (1,907.40 km2) in [Wisconsin's state forests](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_state_forests "List of Wisconsin state forests").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

### Climate

Further information: [Climate change in Wisconsin](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Wisconsin "Climate change in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Wisconsin_K%C3%B6ppen.svg/250px-Wisconsin_K%C3%B6ppen.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_K%C3%B6ppen.svg)

Köppen climate types of Wisconsin

Most of Wisconsin is classified as warm-summer [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfb*), while southern and southwestern portions are classified as hot-summer humid continental climate (Köppen *Dfa*). The highest temperature ever recorded in the state was in the Wisconsin Dells, on July 13, 1936, where it reached 114 °F (46 °C). The lowest temperature ever recorded in Wisconsin was in the village of [Couderay](/wiki/Couderay "Couderay"), where it reached −55 °F (−48 °C) on both February 2 and 4, 1996. Wisconsin also receives a large amount of regular snowfall averaging around 40 inches (100 cm) in the southern portions with up to 160 inches (410 cm) annually in the Lake Superior [snowbelt](/wiki/Snowbelt "Snowbelt") each year.[[97]](#cite_note-uwexclimate-98)

### Cities

Further information: [List of municipalities in Wisconsin by population](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Wisconsin_by_population "List of municipalities in Wisconsin by population") and [Administrative divisions of Wisconsin](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Wisconsin_municipalities.svg/250px-Wisconsin_municipalities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_municipalities.svg)

[Wisconsin Municipalities](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") map of [counties](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_Wisconsin "List of counties in Wisconsin"), [cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wisconsin "List of cities in Wisconsin"), [villages](/wiki/List_of_villages_in_Wisconsin "List of villages in Wisconsin"), and [towns](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Wisconsin "List of towns in Wisconsin").

Wisconsin has three types of municipality: cities, villages, and towns. [Cities](/wiki/City_(Wisconsin) "City (Wisconsin)") and [villages](/wiki/Village_(Wisconsin) "Village (Wisconsin)") are incorporated urban areas. [Towns](/wiki/Town_(Wisconsin) "Town (Wisconsin)") are unincorporated [minor civil divisions](/wiki/Minor_civil_division "Minor civil division") of [counties](/wiki/Political_subdivisions_of_Wisconsin#County "Political subdivisions of Wisconsin") with limited self-government.

Over two-thirds of Wisconsin residents live in urban areas.[[99]](#cite_note-wi2000-100) [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), in southeastern Wisconsin, is the state's most populous city, with approximately 580,000 people. The [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area") accounts for 1.57 million of the state's residents. With a population of nearly 280,000, the state capital of [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin") is consistently ranked as one of the most livable cities in both the state and country and is the [fastest-growing](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") city in Wisconsin.[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102) The [Madison metropolitan area](/wiki/Madison_metropolitan_area "Madison metropolitan area") in southern Wisconsin has about 680,000 residents.

Medium-size cities dot the state. The largest of these is [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") in northeastern Wisconsin, with approximately 320,000 people in the metro area. Other metropolitan cities in the state include [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin"), [Racine](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin"), [Oshkosh](/wiki/Oshkosh,_Wisconsin "Oshkosh, Wisconsin"), [Eau Claire](/wiki/Eau_Claire,_Wisconsin "Eau Claire, Wisconsin"), [Janesville](/wiki/Janesville,_Wisconsin "Janesville, Wisconsin"), [Wausau](/wiki/Wausau,_Wisconsin "Wausau, Wisconsin"), [La Crosse](/wiki/La_Crosse,_Wisconsin "La Crosse, Wisconsin"), [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin"), and [Fond du Lac](/wiki/Fond_du_Lac,_Wisconsin "Fond du Lac, Wisconsin"). Furthermore, another 12 cities function as centers of [micropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_areas "Micropolitan statistical areas") which typically anchor a network of working farms surrounding them. As of 2011, there were 12 cities in Wisconsin with a population of 50,000 or more, accounting for 73% of the state's employment.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Wisconsin demographics for 2024

The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") estimated Wisconsin's population at 5,822,434 on July 1, 2019, a 2.4% increase since the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[106]](#cite_note-PopEstUS-107) This included a natural increase since the last census of 150,659 people (i.e., 614,771 births minus 464,112 deaths) and a decrease due to net migration of 12,755 people. Immigration resulted in a net increase of 59,251 people, and migration from within the U.S. resulted in a net decrease of 72,006 people.[[107]](#cite_note-Components-108)

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 4,775 [homeless](/wiki/Homeless "Homeless") people in Wisconsin.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

In 2022, the five largest European ancestry groups were: [German](/wiki/German_American "German American") (36%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (10.2%), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American") (7.9%), [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") (6.7%), and [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_American "Norwegian American") (6.3%).[[118]](#cite_note-120) German is the most common ancestry in every county in the state, except Menominee, Trempealeau, and Vernon.[[119]](#cite_note-121) Wisconsin has the highest percentage of residents of Polish ancestry of any state.[[120]](#cite_note-census.gov-122) According to the 2022 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), 7.6% of Wisconsin's population were of [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") origin (of any race). The largest Hispanic ancestry groups were [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") (5.1%), [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans") (1.1%), [Central American](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") (0.4%), and [Cuban](/wiki/Cuban_American "Cuban American") (0.1%), with 0.9% reporting other Hispanic or Latino origins.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

Since its founding, Wisconsin has been ethnically heterogeneous. Following the period of French fur traders, the next wave of settlers were miners, many of whom were [Cornish](/wiki/Cornish_American "Cornish American"), who settled the southwestern area of the state. The next wave was dominated by "Yankees", migrants of [English descent](/wiki/English_American "English American") from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"); in the early years of statehood, they dominated the state's heavy industry, finance, politics, and education. Between 1850 and 1900, the immigrants were mostly [Germans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans"), [Scandinavians](/wiki/Scandinavian_Americans "Scandinavian Americans") (the largest group being [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_American "Norwegian American")), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans"), and [Poles](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans"). In the 20th century, a number of African Americans and [Mexicans](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") settled in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"); and after the end of the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") came an influx of [Hmongs](/wiki/Hmong_Americans "Hmong Americans").

The various ethnic groups settled in different areas of the state. Although German immigrants settled throughout the state, the largest concentration was in Milwaukee. Norwegian immigrants settled in lumbering and farming areas in the north and west. Irish, Italian, and Polish immigrants settled primarily in urban areas.[[122]](#cite_note-124) [Menominee County](/wiki/Menominee_County,_Wisconsin "Menominee County, Wisconsin") is the only county in the eastern United States with a Native American majority.

African Americans came to Milwaukee, especially from 1940 on. 86% of Wisconsin's African-American population live in four cities: [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Racine](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin"), [Beloit](/wiki/Beloit,_Wisconsin "Beloit, Wisconsin"), [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha "Kenosha"), with Milwaukee home to nearly three-fourths of the state's black Americans. In the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") region, only Detroit and [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") have a higher percentage of African-American residents.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

About 33% of Wisconsin's Asian population is [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people"), with significant communities in [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Wausau](/wiki/Wausau,_Wisconsin "Wausau, Wisconsin"), [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin"), [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin"), [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin"), [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"), [La Crosse](/wiki/La_Crosse "La Crosse"), [Eau Claire](/wiki/Eau_Claire,_Wisconsin "Eau Claire, Wisconsin"), [Oshkosh](/wiki/Oshkosh,_Wisconsin "Oshkosh, Wisconsin"), and [Manitowoc](/wiki/Manitowoc,_Wisconsin "Manitowoc, Wisconsin").[[124]](#cite_note-126) 61,629 people in Wisconsin, or around 1% of the population, identify as Hmong.[[125]](#cite_note-127)

Of the residents of Wisconsin, 71.7% were born in Wisconsin, 23.0% were born in a different US state, 0.7% were born in Puerto Rico, U.S. Island areas, or born abroad to American parent(s), and 4.6% were foreign born.[[126]](#cite_note-128)

In 2018, the countries of origin for Wisconsin's immigrants came from Mexico, India, China, [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").[[127]](#cite_note-129)
