### Religion

Religious self-identification, per [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2022 *American Values Survey*[[139]](#cite_note-avs-141)

1. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States") (41.0%)
2. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism_in_the_United_States "Catholicism in the United States") (27.0%)
3. [Jehovah's Witness](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (1.00%)
4. [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_the_United_States "Judaism in the United States") (1.00%)
5. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_United_States "Buddhism in the United States") (1.00%)
6. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_States "Hinduism in the United States") (1.00%)
7. [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism") (1.00%)
8. [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") (1.00%)
9. [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_United_States "Irreligion in the United States") (25.0%)
10. Other (1.00%)

According to [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute")'s 2022 *American Values Survey*, those identifying with a religion or spiritual tradition were approximately 75% of the state's population.[[140]](#cite_note-carroll2000-142) 69% of Wisconsinites self-identified as [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian"). Specifically, 25% of respondents identified as [Mainline Protestant](/wiki/Mainline_(Protestant) "Mainline (Protestant)"), 12% as [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), 4% as other Protestants, and 27% as [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"). Roughly 25% of the population were unaffiliated with any religious body. Small minorities of [Jews](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") (1%), [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") (1%), [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (1%), [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (1%), [Unitarian Universalists](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism") (1%), [Muslims](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (<1%), [Mormons](/wiki/Mormonism "Mormonism") (<1%), and other faiths exist according to this study.[[139]](#cite_note-avs-141)

Christianity is the predominant religion in Wisconsin. Per the [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives")' 2020 study, Catholicism was the single-largest denomination with 1,237,342 adherents, followed by the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") with 316,245 members and the [Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod") with 209,788 adherents.[[141]](#cite_note-143) 276,904 adhered to [nondenominational Christianity](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity").

### Crime

Main article: [Crime in Wisconsin](/wiki/Crime_in_Wisconsin "Crime in Wisconsin")

Statewide FBI Crime statistics for 2009 include 144 murders/non-negligent manslaughter; 1,108 rapes; 4,850 robberies; 8,431 aggravated assaults; and 147,486 property crimes.[[142]](#cite_note-144) Wisconsin also publishes its own statistics through the Bureau of Justice Information and Analysis.[[143]](#cite_note-145) The state reported 14,603 violent crimes in 2009, with a clearance rate (% solved) of 50%.[[144]](#cite_note-146) The state reported 4,633 sexual assaults in 2009, with an overall clearance rate for sexual assaults of 57%.

## Economy

See also: [Wisconsin locations by per capita income](/wiki/Wisconsin_locations_by_per_capita_income "Wisconsin locations by per capita income")

In 2023, Wisconsin's [gross state product](/wiki/Gross_state_product "Gross state product") was $413.966 billion, making it [21st among U.S. states](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_GDP "List of U.S. states and territories by GDP").[[145]](#cite_note-147) The economy of Wisconsin is driven by manufacturing, agriculture, and tourism. The [per capita personal income](/wiki/Per_capita_personal_income_in_the_United_States "Per capita personal income in the United States") was $61,475 in 2022 and was $66,596 when adjusted by regional price parity. As of May 2025, the state's unemployment rate was 3.3%.[[146]](#cite_note-148) Since 2009, Wisconsin's [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage") has been $7.25, the same as the federal rate.[[147]](#cite_note-149) In 2025, 99.4% of businesses in Wisconsin were [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business") and they employed 48.0% of the state's workforce.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

Eight corporations based in Wisconsin are listed on the [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500"). In 2024, the list included [Northwestern Mutual](/wiki/Northwestern_Mutual "Northwestern Mutual"), [Fiserv](/wiki/Fiserv "Fiserv"), [ManpowerGroup](/wiki/ManpowerGroup "ManpowerGroup"), [Rockwell Automation](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation "Rockwell Automation"), and [WEC Energy Group](/wiki/WEC_Energy_Group "WEC Energy Group") (all based in Milwaukee), as well as [Kohl's](/wiki/Kohl%27s "Kohl's") (based in [Menomonee Falls](/wiki/Menomonee_Falls,_Wisconsin "Menomonee Falls, Wisconsin")), [American Family Insurance](/wiki/American_Family_Insurance "American Family Insurance") (based in Madison), and [Oshkosh Corporation](/wiki/Oshkosh_Corporation "Oshkosh Corporation") (based in [Oshkosh](/wiki/Oshkosh,_Wisconsin "Oshkosh, Wisconsin")).[[149]](#cite_note-151)

### Manufacturing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/U.S._Bank_Center_%2850144125176%29.jpg/250px-U.S._Bank_Center_%2850144125176%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Bank_Center_(50144125176).jpg)

The [U.S. Bank Center](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Center_(Milwaukee) "U.S. Bank Center (Milwaukee)") in [downtown Milwaukee](/wiki/Downtown_Milwaukee "Downtown Milwaukee") is home to the main headquarters of [Baird](/wiki/Baird_(investment_bank) "Baird (investment bank)"), [Foley & Lardner](/wiki/Foley_%26_Lardner "Foley & Lardner"), and [Sensient Technologies](/wiki/Sensient_Technologies "Sensient Technologies"), as well as regional headquarters for [U.S. Bank](/wiki/U.S._Bank "U.S. Bank") and [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM").

As of 2015, the number of manufacturing jobs in the state was approximately 500,000, similar to the figure in 1970. However, it declined as a share of the state's gross state product between 2000 and 2016 from about 21.5% to 18.5%, a proportion that is eighth among all states.[[150]](#cite_note-WisEconomy-152) Similarly, as a share of total employment, manufacturing declined from 28% in 1970 to 14% in 2015. The state's economic output from manufacturing was $48.9 billion in 2008, making it the tenth largest among states in manufacturing gross domestic product.[[151]](#cite_note-153)

Major Wisconsin companies in manufacturing include the [Kohler Company](/wiki/Kohler_Company "Kohler Company"); [Mercury Marine](/wiki/Mercury_Marine "Mercury Marine"); [Rockwell Automation](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation "Rockwell Automation"); [Johnson Controls](/wiki/Johnson_Controls "Johnson Controls"); [John Deere](/wiki/John_Deere "John Deere"); [Briggs & Stratton](/wiki/Briggs_%26_Stratton "Briggs & Stratton"); [Milwaukee Electric Tool Company](/wiki/Milwaukee_Electric_Tool_Company "Milwaukee Electric Tool Company"); [Miller Electric](/wiki/Miller_Electric "Miller Electric"); [Caterpillar Inc.](/wiki/Caterpillar_Inc. "Caterpillar Inc."); [Joy Global](/wiki/Joy_Global "Joy Global"); [Oshkosh Corporation](/wiki/Oshkosh_Corporation "Oshkosh Corporation"); [Harley-Davidson](/wiki/Harley-Davidson "Harley-Davidson"); [Case IH](/wiki/Case_IH "Case IH"); [S. C. Johnson & Son](/wiki/S._C._Johnson_%26_Son "S. C. Johnson & Son"); [Ashley Furniture](/wiki/Ashley_Furniture "Ashley Furniture"); [Ariens](/wiki/Ariens "Ariens"); and [Evinrude Outboard Motors](/wiki/Evinrude_Outboard_Motors "Evinrude Outboard Motors").

Wisconsin has a significant lumber industry, being a major producer of paper and packaging. Wisconsin ranks first nationwide in the production of paper products; the lower Fox River from [Lake Winnebago](/wiki/Lake_Winnebago "Lake Winnebago") to [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_(Lake_Michigan) "Green Bay (Lake Michigan)") has 24 [paper mills](/wiki/Paper_mill "Paper mill") along its 39 miles (63 km) stretch.[[152]](#cite_note-154)

A large part of the state's manufacturing sector includes commercial food processing, including well-known brands such as [Oscar Mayer](/wiki/Oscar_Mayer "Oscar Mayer"), [Tombstone](/wiki/Tombstone_(pizza) "Tombstone (pizza)") frozen pizza, [Johnsonville](/wiki/Johnsonville_Foods "Johnsonville Foods") [brats](/wiki/Bratwurst "Bratwurst"), and [Usinger's sausage](/wiki/Fred_Usinger "Fred Usinger"). [Kraft Foods](/wiki/Kraft_Foods "Kraft Foods") alone employs more than 5,000 people in the state. Milwaukee is a major producer of beer and was formerly headquarters for [Miller Brewing Company](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company")—the nation's second-largest brewer—until it merged with Coors. Formerly, [Schlitz](/wiki/Joseph_Schlitz_Brewing_Company "Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company"), [Blatz](/wiki/Valentin_Blatz_Brewing_Company "Valentin Blatz Brewing Company"), and [Pabst](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company") were cornerstone breweries in Milwaukee.

The development and manufacture of healthcare devices and software is a growing sector of the state's economy, with key players such as [GE Healthcare](/wiki/GE_Healthcare "GE Healthcare"), [Epic Systems](/wiki/Epic_Systems "Epic Systems"), and [TomoTherapy](/wiki/TomoTherapy "TomoTherapy").

### Agriculture

Main article: [Agriculture in Wisconsin](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Wisconsin "Agriculture in Wisconsin")

See also: [Wisconsin dairy industry](/wiki/Wisconsin_dairy_industry "Wisconsin dairy industry") and [Wisconsin cheese](/wiki/Wisconsin_cheese "Wisconsin cheese")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Dunnum_Family%27s_Top_of_the_Town_dairy_farm_near_Westby_in_Vernon_County%2C_Wisconsin.jpg/250px-Dunnum_Family%27s_Top_of_the_Town_dairy_farm_near_Westby_in_Vernon_County%2C_Wisconsin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dunnum_Family%27s_Top_of_the_Town_dairy_farm_near_Westby_in_Vernon_County,_Wisconsin.jpg)

Dairy cows at a [Wisconsin dairy farm](/wiki/Wisconsin_dairy_industry "Wisconsin dairy industry")

Wisconsin produces about a quarter of America's cheese, leading the nation in cheese production.[[153]](#cite_note-USDA-155) It is second in milk production, after [California](/wiki/California "California"),[[154]](#cite_note-156) and third in per-capita milk production, behind California and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont").[[155]](#cite_note-157) Wisconsin is second in butter production, producing about one-quarter of the nation's butter.[[156]](#cite_note-158) Wisconsin requires cheese and butter makers to hold a license for production, being the only state in the US to require certification for either.[[157]](#cite_note-159)

The state ranks first nationally in the production of corn for [silage](/wiki/Silage "Silage"), [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberries "Cranberries"),[[158]](#cite_note-USDA_Cran-160) [ginseng](/wiki/Ginseng "Ginseng"),[[159]](#cite_note-USDA_Gin-161) and [snap beans](/wiki/Snap_beans "Snap beans") for processing. It grows more than half the national crop of cranberries.[[158]](#cite_note-USDA_Cran-160) and 97% of the nation's ginseng.[[159]](#cite_note-USDA_Gin-161) Wisconsin is also a leading producer of [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat"), potatoes, carrots, tart [cherries](/wiki/Cherries "Cherries"), [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup"), and [sweet corn](/wiki/Sweet_corn "Sweet corn") for processing.

The significance of the state's agricultural production is exemplified by the depiction of a Holstein cow, an ear of corn, and a wheel of cheese on Wisconsin's [state quarter](/wiki/State_quarter "State quarter") design.[[160]](#cite_note-162) The state annually selects an "[Alice in Dairyland](/wiki/Alice_in_Dairyland "Alice in Dairyland")" to promote the state's agricultural products around the world.[[161]](#cite_note-163) The prominence of the dairy industry in Wisconsin has led to Wisconsin being known as "America's Dairyland",[[162]](#cite_note-164)[[6]](#cite_note-Urdang-6) which was made the official state slogan in 1940.[[163]](#cite_note-165)

### Tourism

Further information: [Economy of Door County, Wisconsin](/wiki/Economy_of_Door_County,_Wisconsin "Economy of Door County, Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Wisconsin_Dells%2C_Memorial_Day_Weekend_2014.jpg/250px-Wisconsin_Dells%2C_Memorial_Day_Weekend_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_Dells,_Memorial_Day_Weekend_2014.jpg)

The [Dells of the Wisconsin River](/wiki/Dells_of_the_Wisconsin_River "Dells of the Wisconsin River") form a tourism region focused on river features and nearby [theme parks](/wiki/Theme_park "Theme park").

According to the Department of Tourism, tourism in Wisconsin generated $20.9 billion in total economic impact in 2021 and is the state's third-largest sector.[[164]](#cite_note-witour-166) Festivals such as [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest") and the [EAA AirVenture Oshkosh](/wiki/EAA_AirVenture_Oshkosh "EAA AirVenture Oshkosh") draw international attention, along with hundreds of thousands of visitors annually.[[165]](#cite_note-167) Notable attractions across the state include the [Harley-Davidson Museum](/wiki/Harley-Davidson_Museum "Harley-Davidson Museum"), [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field"), [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum"), [Chazen Museum of Art](/wiki/Chazen_Museum_of_Art "Chazen Museum of Art"), [Henry Vilas Zoo](/wiki/Henry_Vilas_Zoo "Henry Vilas Zoo"), [National Railroad Museum](/wiki/National_Railroad_Museum "National Railroad Museum"), and [Wisconsin State Capital](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Capital "Wisconsin State Capital").[[166]](#cite_note-168) Other tourist destinations include [Taliesin](/wiki/Taliesin_(studio) "Taliesin (studio)"), the studio of architect [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright"); [House on the Rock](/wiki/House_on_the_Rock "House on the Rock"), a complex of architecturally distinct rooms; and the [Circus World Museum](/wiki/Circus_World_Museum "Circus World Museum"), located in the [Ringling brothers](/wiki/Ringling_brothers "Ringling brothers")' hometown of [Baraboo](/wiki/Baraboo,_Wisconsin "Baraboo, Wisconsin").

The [Dells of the Wisconsin River](/wiki/Dells_of_the_Wisconsin_River "Dells of the Wisconsin River"), a gorge noted for its rock formations in south-central Wisconsin, annually attracts more than four million visitors between water tours of the scenery and numerous [theme parks](/wiki/Theme_park "Theme park") in the region.[[164]](#cite_note-witour-166) Nearby [Devil's Lake State Park](/wiki/Devil%27s_Lake_State_Park_(Wisconsin) "Devil's Lake State Park (Wisconsin)") is one of the most visited of [Wisconsin's state parks](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_state_parks "List of Wisconsin state parks") due to its proximity to the Dells and its own scenery.

The [Door Peninsula](/wiki/Door_Peninsula "Door Peninsula"), which extends off the eastern coast of the state, contains [Door County](/wiki/Door_County,_Wisconsin "Door County, Wisconsin"), a popular destination for boaters due to the large number of natural harbors and boat launches on both the Green Bay and Lake Michigan sides of the peninsula. The area draws more than two million visitors yearly to its quaint villages, seasonal cherry picking, and [fish boils](/wiki/Fish_boil "Fish boil").[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)

Given the large number of lakes and rivers in the state, water recreation is popular. In the Northwoods Lake Country, what had been an industrial area focused on timber has largely been transformed into a vacation destination. Popular interest in the environment and environmentalism, added to traditional interests in hunting and fishing, has attracted a large urban audience within driving range.[[169]](#cite_note-171) [Lake Geneva](/wiki/Lake_Geneva,_Wisconsin "Lake Geneva, Wisconsin") in southeastern Wisconsin is similarly popular for water recreation.

### Energy

See also: [Focus on Energy](/wiki/Focus_on_Energy "Focus on Energy"), [Wind power in Wisconsin](/wiki/Wind_power_in_Wisconsin "Wind power in Wisconsin"), and [Solar power in Wisconsin](/wiki/Solar_power_in_Wisconsin "Solar power in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Wisconsin_power_grid.webp/250px-Wisconsin_power_grid.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_power_grid.webp)

[Wisconsin power grid](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Wisconsin "List of power stations in Wisconsin")

Wisconsin has no production of oil, gas, or coal.[[170]](#cite_note-eia-sedb-172) Its in-state electrical generation is mostly from coal. Other important electricity sources are natural gas and nuclear.[[170]](#cite_note-eia-sedb-172)

The state has a mandate that ten percent of its electrical energy come from renewable sources by the end of 2015.[[171]](#cite_note-173) This goal has been met, but not with in-state sources. As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin&action=edit), a third of that ten percent comes from out-of-state sources, mostly wind-generated electricity from Minnesota and Iowa. The state has agnostic policies for developing wind power in state.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

### Taxation

Main articles: [Sales taxes in the United States](/wiki/Sales_taxes_in_the_United_States "Sales taxes in the United States"), [Property tax in the United States](/wiki/Property_tax_in_the_United_States "Property tax in the United States"), and [State income tax](/wiki/State_income_tax "State income tax")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Wisconsin_Budget_%282021%29.webp/330px-Wisconsin_Budget_%282021%29.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_Budget_(2021).webp)

Wisconsin Budget (2021)

Wisconsin collects personal [income taxes](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") which range from 4% to 7.65% based on five [income brackets](/wiki/Income_bracket "Income bracket"). The state [sales](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") and [use tax](/wiki/Use_tax "Use tax") rate is 5.0%. Fifty-nine counties have an additional sales/use tax of 0.5%.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

The most common property tax assessed on Wisconsin residents is the real [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax"), or their residential property tax. Wisconsin does not impose a property tax on vehicles, but does levy an annual registration fee. Property taxes are the most important tax revenue source for Wisconsin's local governments, as well as major methods of funding school districts, vocational-technical colleges, special purpose districts and tax incremental finance districts. Equalized values are based on the full market value of all taxable property in the state, except for agricultural land. To provide property tax relief for farmers, the value of agricultural land is determined by its value for agricultural uses, rather than for its possible development value. Equalized values are used to distribute state aid payments to counties, municipalities, and technical colleges. Assessments prepared by local assessors are used to distribute the property tax burden within individual municipalities.

Wisconsin does not assess a tax on [intangible property](/wiki/Intangible_property "Intangible property"). Wisconsin does not collect [inheritance taxes](/wiki/Inheritance_tax "Inheritance tax"). Until January 1, 2008, Wisconsin's [estate tax](/wiki/Estate_tax "Estate tax") was decoupled from the federal estate tax laws; therefore the state imposed its own estate tax on certain large estates.[[174]](#cite_note-176)

There are no [toll roads](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road") in Wisconsin; highway construction and maintenance are funded in part by [motor fuel tax](/wiki/Motor_fuel_tax "Motor fuel tax") revenues, and the remaining balance is drawn from the State General Fund. Non-highway road construction and maintenance are funded by local governments (municipalities or counties).

## Culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/JO_PARIS_2024_PENTATHLON_MODERNE_MENS_PARC_DU_CHATEAU_DE_VERSAILLES_-_53915642596.jpg/250px-JO_PARIS_2024_PENTATHLON_MODERNE_MENS_PARC_DU_CHATEAU_DE_VERSAILLES_-_53915642596.jpg)](/wiki/File:JO_PARIS_2024_PENTATHLON_MODERNE_MENS_PARC_DU_CHATEAU_DE_VERSAILLES_-_53915642596.jpg)

[Cheesehead](/wiki/Cheesehead "Cheesehead") hats at the [2024 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2024_Summer_Olympics "2024 Summer Olympics")

See also: [History of cheesemaking in Wisconsin](/wiki/History_of_cheesemaking_in_Wisconsin "History of cheesemaking in Wisconsin")

Residents of Wisconsin are referred to as Wisconsinites. The traditional prominence of references to [dairy farming](/wiki/Dairy_farming "Dairy farming") and [cheesemaking](/wiki/Cheesemaking "Cheesemaking") in Wisconsin's rural economy (the state's [license plates](/wiki/License_plates "License plates") have read "America's Dairyland" since 1940)[[175]](#cite_note-177) have led to the nickname (sometimes used pejoratively among non-residents) of "[cheeseheads](/wiki/Cheesehead "Cheesehead")", and to the creation of "cheesehead hats" made of yellow foam in the shape of a wedge of cheese.

Numerous [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic "Ethnic") festivals are held throughout Wisconsin to celebrate the heritage of its citizens. Such festivals include [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest"), [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest_celebrations "Oktoberfest celebrations"), [Polish Fest](/wiki/Polish_Fest "Polish Fest"), [Festa Italiana](/wiki/Festa_Italiana "Festa Italiana"), [Irish Fest](/wiki/Irish_Fest "Irish Fest"), Bastille Days, [Syttende Mai](/wiki/Syttende_Mai "Syttende Mai") (Norwegian Constitution Day), Brat(wurst) Days in [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin"), [Polka Days](/wiki/Pulaski,_Wisconsin#Pulaski_Polka_Days "Pulaski, Wisconsin"), Cheese Days in [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe,_Wisconsin "Monroe, Wisconsin") and [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon "Mequon"), African World Festival, Indian Summer, Arab Fest, Wisconsin Highland Games, and many others.[[176]](#cite_note-178)

### Architecture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Taliesin_Courtyard_02.jpg/250px-Taliesin_Courtyard_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taliesin_Courtyard_02.jpg)

[Taliesin](/wiki/Taliesin_(studio) "Taliesin (studio)") was the studio of architect [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright").

With the immigration of [northern Europeans](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans") into Wisconsin and the [upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"), they brought the techniques of building [log homes](/wiki/Log_house "Log house") with them.[[177]](#cite_note-179)

The [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum") is known for its Quadracci Pavilion created by Spanish architect [Santiago Calatrava](/wiki/Santiago_Calatrava "Santiago Calatrava"). The Quadracci Pavilion contains a movable, wing-like Burke *[brise soleil](/wiki/Brise_soleil "Brise soleil")* that opens up for a wingspan of 217 feet (66 m) during the day, folding over the tall, arched structure at night or during inclement weather.[[178]](#cite_note-180)

[Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright"), an architect known as the pioneer of [Prairie School](/wiki/Prairie_School "Prairie School") architecture and the [Usonian](/wiki/Usonia "Usonia") home concept, was raised in Wisconsin. Wright's home and studio in the 20th century was at [Taliesin](/wiki/Taliesin_(studio) "Taliesin (studio)"), south of [Spring Green, Wisconsin](/wiki/Spring_Green,_Wisconsin "Spring Green, Wisconsin"). Taliesin and the Usonian [Jacobs I House](/wiki/Herbert_and_Katherine_Jacobs_First_House "Herbert and Katherine Jacobs First House") in Madison are listed as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") as part of "[The 20th-Century Architecture of Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/The_20th-Century_Architecture_of_Frank_Lloyd_Wright "The 20th-Century Architecture of Frank Lloyd Wright")".[[24]](#cite_note-whs-25) Other notable works of Wright in Wisconsin include the [Annunciation Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Annunciation_Greek_Orthodox_Church "Annunciation Greek Orthodox Church") in Wauwatosa, [First Unitarian Society of Madison](/wiki/First_Unitarian_Society_of_Madison "First Unitarian Society of Madison"), and [Johnson Wax Headquarters](/wiki/Johnson_Wax_Headquarters "Johnson Wax Headquarters"). [Monona Terrace](/wiki/Monona_Terrace "Monona Terrace") in Madison, a convention center designed by Taliesin architect Anthony Puttnam, is based on a 1930s design by Wright.[[179]](#cite_note-181)

### Alcohol

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Miller_Brewery.png/250px-Miller_Brewery.png)](/wiki/File:Miller_Brewery.png)

The [Miller Brewery](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company") in Milwaukee

Drinking has long been considered a significant part of Wisconsin culture, and the state ranks at or near the top of national measures of per-capita alcohol consumption, consumption of alcohol per state, and proportion of drinkers. Consumption per-capita per-event, however, ranks low among the nation; number of events (number of times alcohol is involved) is significantly higher or highest, but consumption at each event smaller, marking Wisconsin's consumption as frequent and moderate.[[180]](#cite_note-182) Factors such as cultural identification with the state's heritage of German immigration, the long-standing presence of major breweries in Milwaukee, and a cold climate are often associated with the prevalence of drinking in Wisconsin.

Many large breweries were founded in Wisconsin, largely in Milwaukee, which gained the epithet "Brew City" before the turn of the century. [Miller Brewing Company](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company"), [Pabst Brewing Company](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company"), [Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company](/wiki/Joseph_Schlitz_Brewing_Company "Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company"), and [Jacob Leinenkugel Brewing Company](/wiki/Jacob_Leinenkugel_Brewing_Company "Jacob Leinenkugel Brewing Company") all began as local businesses before entering national markets. Several other popular craft brews include Ale Asylum,[[181]](#cite_note-183) [Capital](/wiki/Capital_Brewery "Capital Brewery"), [Sprecher](/wiki/Sprecher_Brewery "Sprecher Brewery"), and [New Glarus](/wiki/New_Glarus_Brewing_Company "New Glarus Brewing Company"), the latter being well known for the Spotted Cow Farmhouse Ale only sold in Wisconsin.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

In Wisconsin, the legal drinking age is 21, except when accompanied by a parent, guardian, or spouse who is at least 21 years old. Age requirements are waived for possessing alcohol when employed by a brewer, brewpub, wholesaler, or producer of alcohol fuel. The minimum legal age to purchase alcohol is 21, with no exceptions.[[183]](#cite_note-185) The Absolute Sobriety law states that any person not of legal drinking age (currently 21) may not drive after consuming alcohol.[[184]](#cite_note-186) DUI offenses were lowered to BAC 0.08 in 2003 as a result of federal government pressure.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Cuisine of Wisconsin](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Wisconsin "Cuisine of Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Fried_Cheese_Curds_Green_Bay_Wisconsin.JPG/250px-Fried_Cheese_Curds_Green_Bay_Wisconsin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fried_Cheese_Curds_Green_Bay_Wisconsin.JPG)

Fried [cheese curds](/wiki/Cheese_curd "Cheese curd")

Wisconsin's cuisine is famous for [its cheese](/wiki/Wisconsin_cheese "Wisconsin cheese") as well as other dairy products. [Colby cheese](/wiki/Colby_cheese "Colby cheese") was first created in Wisconsin in 1885 (named after [Colby, Wisconsin](/wiki/Colby,_Wisconsin "Colby, Wisconsin")), and [brick cheese](/wiki/Brick_cheese "Brick cheese") was first created in the state in 1877.[[186]](#cite_note-norton2009-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189) The state is the only producer of [Limburger cheese](/wiki/Limburger_cheese "Limburger cheese") in the United States. [Cheese curds](/wiki/Cheese_curd "Cheese curd") are a popular variant that can be eaten separately cold as a snack, or covered in batter and fried as an appetizer, often served with [ranch dressing](/wiki/Ranch_dressing "Ranch dressing") as a dipping sauce. [Hot and spicy cheese bread](/wiki/Hot_and_spicy_cheese_bread "Hot and spicy cheese bread") is a popular type of bread created and sold in Madison.

Wisconsin is the nation's top producer of [cheese](/wiki/Cheese "Cheese"), the state's official dairy product. It is also a leading producer of [cranberries](/wiki/Cranberries "Cranberries"), the state's official fruit, and [ginseng](/wiki/Ginseng "Ginseng").[[188]](#cite_note-190) [Dane County Farmers' Market](/wiki/Dane_County_Farmers%27_Market "Dane County Farmers' Market") in Madison is the nation's largest producers-only farmer's market.[[189]](#cite_note-BadgerHeraldReidy-191)[[190]](#cite_note-WaPoWax-192)

The Friday night [fish fry](/wiki/Fish_fry "Fish fry"), often battered and fried [perch](/wiki/Perch "Perch") or [walleye](/wiki/Walleye "Walleye"), is traditional throughout Wisconsin, while in northeast Wisconsin and [Door County](/wiki/Door_County,_Wisconsin "Door County, Wisconsin") the [fish boil](/wiki/Fish_boil "Fish boil") is more popular. The [supper club](/wiki/Supper_club "Supper club") is another common phenomenon of Wisconsin culinary heritage and often a destination for fish frys; other fried food are common side dishes, along with condiments of tartar sauce and [cole slaw](/wiki/Cole_slaw "Cole slaw") or crimson slaw, a variety of cole slaw that incorporates Wisconsin cranberries.[[191]](#cite_note-193) [Beer cheese soup](/wiki/Beer_soup "Beer soup") is usually made from a variety of beer and cheddar or Colby cheese with sausage, potatoes, and green onions.

[Booyah](/wiki/Booyah_(stew) "Booyah (stew)") is a [stew](/wiki/Stew "Stew") common to Wisconsin, commonly using meat and a mirepoix of vegetables cooked together in a "booyah kettle" over low heat for several days.[[192]](#cite_note-194)

The southeastern city of [Racine](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin") is known for its [kringle](/wiki/Kringle "Kringle"), a sweet flaky pastry often served as a dessert. The recipe was brought by [Danish immigrants](/wiki/Danish_Americans "Danish Americans") to the region in the 1800s and became the official state pastry of Wisconsin in 2013.[[193]](#cite_note-195)[[194]](#cite_note-196) The [Wisconsin State Fair](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Fair "Wisconsin State Fair") is known for its giant [cream puffs](/wiki/Cream_puff "Cream puff").[[195]](#cite_note-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198)

The [butter burger](/wiki/Butter_burger "Butter burger") originated in Wisconsin, most likely in [Solly's Grille](/wiki/Solly%27s_Grille "Solly's Grille") in [Glendale](/wiki/Glendale,_Wisconsin "Glendale, Wisconsin").[[197]](#cite_note-199) [Culver's](/wiki/Culver%27s "Culver's") is a midwestern [fast casual food restaurant chain](/wiki/Fast_casual_restaurant "Fast casual restaurant") originally from [Sauk City](/wiki/Sauk_City,_Wisconsin "Sauk City, Wisconsin") and currently headquartered in [Prairie du Sac](/wiki/Prairie_du_Sac,_Wisconsin "Prairie du Sac, Wisconsin") known for serving butter burgers, fried cheese curds, and frozen custard. [La Croix Sparkling Water](/wiki/La_Croix_Sparkling_Water "La Croix Sparkling Water") originates from [La Crosse](/wiki/La_Crosse,_Wisconsin "La Crosse, Wisconsin").

### Film

Wisconsin has a modest but growing [film industry](/wiki/Film_industry_in_Wisconsin "Film industry in Wisconsin"), supported in part by renewed state incentives in 2025.

### Music

Main article: [Music of Wisconsin](/wiki/Music_of_Wisconsin "Music of Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Generac.jpg/250px-Generac.jpg)](/wiki/File:Generac.jpg)

[Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest") is an annual music festival in downtown Milwaukee.

Milwaukee hosts [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest"), dubbed "The World's Largest Music Festival", every year[[198]](#cite_note-200)[[199]](#cite_note-201) The festival is held at the lakefront [Henry Maier Festival Park](/wiki/Henry_Maier_Festival_Park "Henry Maier Festival Park") just south of downtown, as are a summer-long array of [ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic "Ethnic") musical festivals. The [Wisconsin Area Music Industry](/wiki/Wisconsin_Area_Music_Industry "Wisconsin Area Music Industry") hosts an annual awards show for top Wisconsin artists.[[200]](#cite_note-202) The [Mile of Music](/wiki/Mile_of_Music "Mile of Music") in [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin") is an annual all-original music festival of varying styles. It focuses on undiscovered touring musicians and bands.[[201]](#cite_note-203) Other music festivals include [Hoofbeat](/wiki/Hoofbeat_(festival) "Hoofbeat (festival)") country fest, Country Jam USA, the [Hodag Country Festival](/wiki/Hodag_Country_Festival "Hodag Country Festival"), [Lifest](/wiki/Lifest "Lifest"), Porterfield Country Music Festival, Country Thunder USA in Twin Lakes,[[202]](#cite_note-Country_Music_Chicago-204) and Country USA.

### Recreation

See also: [List of Wisconsin amusement parks](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_amusement_parks "List of Wisconsin amusement parks")

The varied landscape of Wisconsin makes the state a popular vacation destination for outdoor recreation. Winter events include skiing, ice fishing and [snowmobile derbies](/wiki/World_Championship_Snowmobile_Derby "World Championship Snowmobile Derby"). Wisconsin is situated on two Great Lakes and has many inland lakes of varied size; the state contains 11,188 square miles (28,980 km2) of water, more than all but three other states—[Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), Michigan, and Florida.[[203]](#cite_note-205) The [Wisconsin Shipwreck Coast National Marine Sanctuary](/wiki/Wisconsin_Shipwreck_Coast_National_Marine_Sanctuary "Wisconsin Shipwreck Coast National Marine Sanctuary") was established in 2021 in the waters of Lake Michigan off Wisconsin and is the site of a large number of historically significant [shipwrecks](/wiki/Shipwreck "Shipwreck").[[204]](#cite_note-206)[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208)

Outdoor activities are popular in Wisconsin, especially hunting and fishing. One of the most prevalent game animals is the [whitetail deer](/wiki/Whitetail_deer "Whitetail deer"). Each year in Wisconsin, well over 600,000 deer-hunting licenses are sold.[[207]](#cite_note-wdnr2008-209) In 2008, the [Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Natural_Resources "Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources") projected the pre-hunt deer population to be between 1.5 and 1.7 million.

### Sports

Main article: [Sports in Wisconsin](/wiki/Sports_in_Wisconsin "Sports in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Lambeau_Field_bowl.jpg/250px-Lambeau_Field_bowl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lambeau_Field_bowl.jpg)

[Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field") is home to the publicly owned [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/F1_V10_%2853742784505%29.jpg/250px-F1_V10_%2853742784505%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:F1_V10_(53742784505).jpg)

[Road America](/wiki/Road_America "Road America"), near [Elkhart Lake](/wiki/Elkhart_Lake,_Wisconsin "Elkhart Lake, Wisconsin"), has hosted [motorsport](/wiki/Motorsport "Motorsport") races since the 1950s.

Wisconsin is represented by major league teams in three sports: football, baseball, and basketball. [Lambeau Field](/wiki/Lambeau_Field "Lambeau Field"), located in [Green Bay, Wisconsin](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin"), is home to the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"). The Packers have been part of the NFL since the league's second season in 1921 and are the smallest city franchise in the NFL, and the only one owned by shareholders statewide.[[208]](#cite_note-210) The [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers"), the state's only major league baseball team, have played in [American Family Field](/wiki/American_Family_Field "American Family Field") in Milwaukee since 2001. Before the Brewers, Milwaukee had two prior Major League teams. The first team, also called the Brewers, played only one season in 1901 before becoming the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns"). Milwaukee was also the home of the now-[Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") franchise when they moved from Boston from 1953 to 1965, winning the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") in 1957.[[209]](#cite_note-211) The [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") play home games at the [Fiserv Forum](/wiki/Fiserv_Forum "Fiserv Forum").[[210]](#cite_note-212)

The state also has minor league teams in hockey ([Milwaukee Admirals](/wiki/Milwaukee_Admirals "Milwaukee Admirals")) and baseball (the [Wisconsin Timber Rattlers](/wiki/Wisconsin_Timber_Rattlers "Wisconsin Timber Rattlers"), based in [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin") and the [Beloit Sky Carp](/wiki/Beloit_Sky_Carp "Beloit Sky Carp") of the [High-A](/wiki/High-A "High-A") minor leagues). In addition to these affiliated minor league teams, Wisconsin has two [American Association of Professional Baseball](/wiki/American_Association_of_Professional_Baseball "American Association of Professional Baseball") teams, being the [Milwaukee Milkmen](/wiki/Milwaukee_Milkmen "Milwaukee Milkmen") based in [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin,_Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Franklin, Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"),[[211]](#cite_note-213) and the [Lake Country DockHounds](/wiki/Lake_Country_DockHounds "Lake Country DockHounds") based in [Oconomowoc](/wiki/Oconomowoc,_Wisconsin "Oconomowoc, Wisconsin").[[212]](#cite_note-214) Wisconsin is also home to nine [Northwoods League](/wiki/Northwoods_League "Northwoods League") teams. The [Madison Mallards](/wiki/Madison_Mallards "Madison Mallards"), the [La Crosse Loggers](/wiki/La_Crosse_Loggers "La Crosse Loggers"), the [Lakeshore Chinooks](/wiki/Lakeshore_Chinooks "Lakeshore Chinooks"), the [Eau Claire Express](/wiki/Eau_Claire_Express "Eau Claire Express"), the [Fond du Lac Dock Spiders](/wiki/Fond_du_Lac_Dock_Spiders "Fond du Lac Dock Spiders"), the [Green Bay Rockers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Rockers "Green Bay Rockers"), the [Kenosha Kingfish](/wiki/Kenosha_Kingfish "Kenosha Kingfish"), the [Wausau Woodchucks](/wiki/Wausau_Woodchucks "Wausau Woodchucks"), and the [Wisconsin Rapids Rafters](/wiki/Wisconsin_Rapids_Rafters "Wisconsin Rapids Rafters") all play in a collegiate all-star summer league. In addition to the Packers, Green Bay is also the home to an [indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") team, the [Green Bay Blizzard](/wiki/Green_Bay_Blizzard "Green Bay Blizzard") of the [IFL](/wiki/Indoor_Football_League "Indoor Football League"). The state is home to the seven-time MISL/MASL Champion [Milwaukee Wave](/wiki/Milwaukee_Wave "Milwaukee Wave").[[213]](#cite_note-215) Wisconsin is also home to [Forward Madison FC](/wiki/Forward_Madison_FC "Forward Madison FC"), which is a professional soccer team that plays in the [USL League One](/wiki/USL_League_One "USL League One"). The Northern Elite Football League consists of many amateur semi-pro teams from Wisconsin.

Wisconsin also has many [college sports](/wiki/College_sports "College sports") programs, including the [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [Wisconsin Badgers](/wiki/Wisconsin_Badgers "Wisconsin Badgers"), [Milwaukee Panthers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Panthers "Milwaukee Panthers"), and [Green Bay Phoenix](/wiki/Green_Bay_Phoenix "Green Bay Phoenix"). The [Marquette Golden Eagles](/wiki/Marquette_Golden_Eagles "Marquette Golden Eagles") of the [Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference_(1979%E2%80%932013) "Big East Conference (1979–2013)"), the state's other major collegiate program, is known for its [men's basketball team](/wiki/Marquette_Golden_Eagles_men%27s_basketball "Marquette Golden Eagles men's basketball"). Many other schools in the University of Wisconsin system compete in the [Wisconsin Intercollegiate Athletic Conference](/wiki/Wisconsin_Intercollegiate_Athletic_Conference "Wisconsin Intercollegiate Athletic Conference") at the [Division III](/wiki/Division_III_(NCAA) "Division III (NCAA)") level. The conference is one of the most successful in the nation, claiming 107 NCAA national championships in 15 different sports as of March 30, 2015.[[214]](#cite_note-216)

The [Milwaukee Mile](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mile "Milwaukee Mile"), an oval track opened in 1903, is the oldest operating motorsports venue in the world, having hosted the [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series") and [NASCAR Xfinity Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Xfinity_Series "NASCAR Xfinity Series").[[215]](#cite_note-217) [Road America](/wiki/Road_America "Road America") near [Elkhart Lake](/wiki/Elkhart_Lake,_Wisconsin "Elkhart Lake, Wisconsin") hosts races in the [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series"), [IMSA SportsCar Championship](/wiki/IMSA_SportsCar_Championship "IMSA SportsCar Championship"), [Sports Car Club of America](/wiki/Sports_Car_Club_of_America "Sports Car Club of America") [GT World Challenge America](/wiki/GT_World_Challenge_America "GT World Challenge America") and [Trans-Am Series](/wiki/Trans-Am_Series "Trans-Am Series") and the [MotoAmerica](/wiki/MotoAmerica "MotoAmerica") Superbike Championship as of 2024. The [World Championship Snowmobile Derby](/wiki/World_Championship_Snowmobile_Derby "World Championship Snowmobile Derby") is held at [Eagle River, Wisconsin](/wiki/Eagle_River,_Wisconsin "Eagle River, Wisconsin"). The world championship [off-road racing](/wiki/Off-road_racing "Off-road racing") event is held at [Crandon International Off-Road Raceway](/wiki/Crandon_International_Off-Road_Raceway "Crandon International Off-Road Raceway").

Wisconsin is home to the nation's oldest operating [velodrome](/wiki/Velodrome "Velodrome") in [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha "Kenosha") where races have been held every year since 1927.[[216]](#cite_note-218)

[Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin") is home to [Whistling Straits](/wiki/Whistling_Straits "Whistling Straits") golf club which has hosted [PGA Championships](/wiki/PGA_Championship "PGA Championship") in 2004, 2010 and 2015 and the [Ryder Cup](/wiki/Ryder_Cup "Ryder Cup") golf competition between USA and Europe in 2020.[[217]](#cite_note-219) The [Greater Milwaukee Open](/wiki/Greater_Milwaukee_Open "Greater Milwaukee Open"), later named the [U.S. Bank Championship in Milwaukee](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Championship_in_Milwaukee "U.S. Bank Championship in Milwaukee"), was a PGA Tour tournament from 1968 to 2009 held annually in [Brown Deer](/wiki/Brown_Deer "Brown Deer"). In 2017, [Erin Hills](/wiki/Erin_Hills "Erin Hills"), a golf course in [Erin, Wisconsin](/wiki/Erin,_Wisconsin "Erin, Wisconsin"), approximately 30 miles northwest of Milwaukee, hosted the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)").[[218]](#cite_note-ctmchges-220)

## Government and politics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Wisconsin_State_Capitol%2C_Christmas_Eve%2C_2012.jpg/250px-Wisconsin_State_Capitol%2C_Christmas_Eve%2C_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_State_Capitol,_Christmas_Eve,_2012.jpg)

The [Wisconsin State Capitol](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Capitol "Wisconsin State Capitol") is located on the [isthmus](/wiki/Isthmus "Isthmus") between [Lake Mendota](/wiki/Lake_Mendota "Lake Mendota") and [Lake Monona](/wiki/Lake_Monona "Lake Monona"), in the city of [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin").

The [Constitution of Wisconsin](/wiki/Constitution_of_Wisconsin "Constitution of Wisconsin") outlines the structure and function of state government, which is organized into three branches: executive, legislative, and judicial. The [Wisconsin Blue Book](/wiki/Wisconsin_Blue_Book "Wisconsin Blue Book") is the primary published reference about the government and politics of the state. Re-published every two years, copies are available from state legislators.[[219]](#cite_note-221) The law of the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") also applies within the [Menominee Indian Reservation](/wiki/Menominee_Indian_Reservation "Menominee Indian Reservation").

### State government

See also: [List of Wisconsin state agencies](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_state_agencies "List of Wisconsin state agencies")

The executive branch is headed by the [Governor of Wisconsin](/wiki/Governor_of_Wisconsin "Governor of Wisconsin"). The current governor, [Tony Evers](/wiki/Tony_Evers "Tony Evers"), assumed office on January 7, 2019. The Wisconsin Constitution grants the governor a veto on bills passed by the state legislature as well as a [line-item veto](/wiki/Line-item_veto "Line-item veto") on appropriation bills. A [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Wisconsin "Lieutenant Governor of Wisconsin") succeeds the governor in the event of any removal from office and performs any duties assigned by the governor. The current lieutenant governor is [Sara Rodriguez](/wiki/Sara_Rodriguez "Sara Rodriguez"). The other elected constitutional offices in the executive branch are the [secretary of state](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_of_Wisconsin "Secretary of State of Wisconsin") ([Sarah Godlewski](/wiki/Sarah_Godlewski "Sarah Godlewski")), [treasurer](/wiki/State_Treasurer_of_Wisconsin "State Treasurer of Wisconsin") ([John Leiber](/wiki/John_Leiber "John Leiber")), [attorney general](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Wisconsin "Attorney General of Wisconsin") ([Josh Kaul](/wiki/Josh_Kaul "Josh Kaul")), and the non-partisan [superintendent of public instruction](/wiki/Superintendent_of_Public_Instruction_of_Wisconsin "Superintendent of Public Instruction of Wisconsin") ([Jill Underly](/wiki/Jill_Underly "Jill Underly")).[[220]](#cite_note-222)[[221]](#cite_note-223)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Secretary_Pompeo_Delivers_Remarks_in_the_Senate_Chamber_of_the_Wisconsin_State_Capitol_%2850378281008%29.jpg/250px-Secretary_Pompeo_Delivers_Remarks_in_the_Senate_Chamber_of_the_Wisconsin_State_Capitol_%2850378281008%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Secretary_Pompeo_Delivers_Remarks_in_the_Senate_Chamber_of_the_Wisconsin_State_Capitol_(50378281008).jpg)

The [Senate Chamber](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate") of the Wisconsin State Capitol

The [Wisconsin State Legislature](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Legislature "Wisconsin State Legislature") is Wisconsin's legislative branch. The Legislature is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") body consisting of the [Wisconsin State Assembly](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Assembly "Wisconsin State Assembly") and the [Wisconsin State Senate](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate"). The Assembly has 99 members, and the Senate has 33 members. All 99 members of the Wisconsin Assembly are elected in a two-year term cycle without [term limits](/wiki/Term_limits_in_the_United_States "Term limits in the United States"). Similarly, all 33 members of the Wisconsin Senate are elected in a four-year cycle, also without term limits.[[222]](#cite_note-legislative-224) Half of the Senate is elected every two years. Members of both houses of the Legislature vote within their ranks to select presiding officers, such as the [Speaker of the Assembly](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") and the [President of the Senate](/wiki/President_of_the_Senate "President of the Senate"). Legislators in both the Senate and the Assembly receive an annual salary of $55,141. Over two years, each legislator is allotted $66,008 to cover general office expenses, printing, postage and district mailings.[[223]](#cite_note-225)

Wisconsin's court system has four levels: municipal courts, circuit courts, the Court of Appeals, and the Supreme Court. Municipal courts typically handle cases involving [local ordinance](/wiki/Local_ordinance "Local ordinance") matters. The [circuit courts](/wiki/Wisconsin_Circuit_Court "Wisconsin Circuit Court") are Wisconsin's [trial courts](/wiki/Trial_courts "Trial courts"), they have [original jurisdiction](/wiki/Original_jurisdiction "Original jurisdiction") in all civil and criminal cases within the state. Challenges to circuit court rulings are heard by the [Wisconsin Court of Appeals](/wiki/Wisconsin_Court_of_Appeals "Wisconsin Court of Appeals"), consisting of sixteen judges who typically sit in three-judge panels. As the state's highest appellate court, the [Wisconsin Supreme Court](/wiki/Wisconsin_Supreme_Court "Wisconsin Supreme Court") may hear both appeals from lower courts and original actions. In addition to deciding cases, the Supreme Court is responsible for administering the state's court system and regulating the practice of law in Wisconsin.[[224]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_Court_System-226)

### Federal representation

Main articles: [Wisconsin's congressional delegations](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_congressional_delegations "Wisconsin's congressional delegations") and [Wisconsin's congressional districts](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_congressional_districts "Wisconsin's congressional districts")

Following the 2020 census [reapportionment](/wiki/United_States_congressional_apportionment "United States congressional apportionment"), Wisconsin has eight seats in the U.S. House of Representatives. As of the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), six federal representatives are Republicans while two are Democrats. [Gwen Moore](/wiki/Gwen_Moore "Gwen Moore") is the most senior member of the Wisconsin delegation to the U.S. House of Representatives, while the least senior is [Tony Wied](/wiki/Tony_Wied "Tony Wied"). Wisconsin's [senior](/wiki/Seniority_in_the_United_States_Senate "Seniority in the United States Senate") [U.S. senator](/wiki/United_States_Senator "United States Senator"), [Ron Johnson](/wiki/Ron_Johnson "Ron Johnson"), is a Republican, while its junior, [Tammy Baldwin](/wiki/Tammy_Baldwin "Tammy Baldwin"), is a Democrat.[[225]](#cite_note-227)

Wisconsin is under the [appellate jurisdiction](/wiki/Appellate_jurisdiction "Appellate jurisdiction") of the [United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Seventh_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit"), which includes district courts for the [Eastern District of Wisconsin](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_Wisconsin "United States District Court for the Eastern District of Wisconsin") and [Western District of Wisconsin](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Western_District_of_Wisconsin "United States District Court for the Western District of Wisconsin").

### Politics

Main article: [Politics of Wisconsin](/wiki/Politics_of_Wisconsin "Politics of Wisconsin")

See also: [United States presidential elections in Wisconsin](/wiki/United_States_presidential_elections_in_Wisconsin "United States presidential elections in Wisconsin") and [Political party strength in Wisconsin](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Wisconsin "Political party strength in Wisconsin")

2024 United States presidential election in Wisconsin

  Results by municipality

Wisconsin is considered a [swing state](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state") at the federal level, being won by either the [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") or [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") nominees.[[226]](#cite_note-228) Republican [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") won the state by 0.87% in the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2024 United States presidential election in Wisconsin"); it had the closest margin of any state in that election. Democrat [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") won the state by a narrow margin of 0.63% in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2020 United States presidential election in Wisconsin");[[227]](#cite_note-229) Trump won Wisconsin in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Wisconsin "2016 United States presidential election in Wisconsin") by a similarly narrow margin of 0.77%, the first time the state voted for a Republican presidential nominee since [1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election "1984 United States presidential election"). Wisconsin was part of the [blue wall](/wiki/Blue_wall_(U.S._politics) "Blue wall (U.S. politics)"), a group of states the Democratic Party won in each presidential election from [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election") to [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election").[[228]](#cite_note-230) Since achieving statehood in 1848, Wisconsin has been won by Republican presidential candidates 26 times, Democrats 18 times, and once by the [Progressive Party](/wiki/Progressive_Party_(United_States,_1924%E2%80%931934) "Progressive Party (United States, 1924–1934)").

At the statewide level, Wisconsin is competitive, with control regularly alternating between the two parties. Following the [2014 general elections](/wiki/2014_Wisconsin_elections "2014 Wisconsin elections"), the governor, lieutenant governor, attorney general, and treasurer were all Republicans, while the secretary of state was a Democrat.[[229]](#cite_note-231) However, in [2018](/wiki/2018_Wisconsin_elections "2018 Wisconsin elections"), Democrats won all constitutional statewide offices on the ballot, the first time this happened in Wisconsin since 1982.[[230]](#cite_note-232) [Among Wisconsin's 46 governors](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Wisconsin "List of governors of Wisconsin"), 32 were Republicans, 12 were Democrats, one was a [Whig](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)"), and two were [Wisconsin Progressive Party](/wiki/Wisconsin_Progressive_Party "Wisconsin Progressive Party") members.

In a 2020 study, Wisconsin was ranked as the 25th easiest state for citizens to vote in.[[231]](#cite_note-233) Some have argued the state has experienced [democratic backsliding](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding") since 2011.[[232]](#cite_note-:2-234)[[233]](#cite_note-:0-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236) Some political scientists classify Wisconsin as a [hybrid regime](/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime");[[232]](#cite_note-:2-234) the state's [House of Representative](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") and [legislature](/wiki/State_legislature_(United_States) "State legislature (United States)") elections are considered to be [free but not fair](/wiki/Free_and_fair_election "Free and fair election"), with districts undergoing "extreme [partisan gerrymanders](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering")" to entrench Republicans "beyond [electoral rotation](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_States "Elections in the United States")".[[232]](#cite_note-:2-234)[[233]](#cite_note-:0-235)[[235]](#cite_note-:1-237) The Wisconsin Supreme Court overturned the legislative gerrymander in the 2023 ruling of *[Clarke v. Wisconsin Elections Commission](/wiki/Clarke_v._Wisconsin_Elections_Commission "Clarke v. Wisconsin Elections Commission").*[[236]](#cite_note-238)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/2011_Wisconsin_Budget_Protests_1_JO.jpg/250px-2011_Wisconsin_Budget_Protests_1_JO.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Wisconsin_Budget_Protests_1_JO.jpg)

The [2011 Wisconsin Act 10](/wiki/2011_Wisconsin_Act_10 "2011 Wisconsin Act 10") led to large protests around the state capitol building in Madison.[[237]](#cite_note-239)

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), Wisconsin was a [historically Republican state](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Republican_Party "History of the United States Republican Party"); in fact, it is the state that gave birth to the Republican Party, although ethno-religious issues in the late 19th century caused a brief split in the coalition. The [Bennett Law](/wiki/Bennett_Law "Bennett Law") campaign of 1890 controversially required English as the sole medium of instruction in all schools, and Germans switched to the Democratic Party because the Republican Party supported the law.[[238]](#cite_note-240)

Wisconsin's political history is broad in scope, encompassing [Robert La Follette](/wiki/Robert_La_Follette "Robert La Follette") and the [Progressive movement](/wiki/Progressive_movement "Progressive movement") to prominent anti-communist [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy"). From the early 20th century, the [Socialist Party of America](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") had a base in Milwaukee. The phenomenon was referred to as "[sewer socialism](/wiki/Sewer_socialism "Sewer socialism")" because the elected officials were more concerned with public works and reform than with revolution (although revolutionary socialism existed in the city as well). Its influence faded in the late 1950s largely because of the [red scare](/wiki/Second_Red_Scare "Second Red Scare") and racial tensions.[[239]](#cite_note-smith2003-241) The first socialist mayor of a large city in the United States was [Emil Seidel](/wiki/Emil_Seidel "Emil Seidel"), elected mayor of Milwaukee in 1910; the city elected three more socialist mayors in [Daniel Hoan](/wiki/Daniel_Hoan "Daniel Hoan"), [Frank P. Zeidler](/wiki/Frank_P._Zeidler "Frank P. Zeidler"), and [Henry Maier](/wiki/Henry_Maier "Henry Maier"). Socialist [newspaper editor](/wiki/Newspaper_editor "Newspaper editor") [Victor Berger](/wiki/Victor_Berger "Victor Berger") was repeatedly elected as a U.S. Representative.

Through the first half of the 20th century, Wisconsin's politics were dominated by Robert La Follette and his sons, originally of the Republican Party and later of the [Wisconsin Progressive Party](/wiki/Wisconsin_Progressive_Party "Wisconsin Progressive Party"). Since 1945, the state has maintained a close balance between Republicans and Democrats.[[240]](#cite_note-conant2006-242) Wisconsin Congressman [Paul Ryan](/wiki/Paul_Ryan "Paul Ryan") was the Republican vice-presidential nominee in the [2012 election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), alongside [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney"),[[241]](#cite_note-243) and later served as 54th [speaker of the House of Representatives](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives").[[242]](#cite_note-244)

### International relations

Wisconsin has sister-state relationships with [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") in Germany, [Chiba Prefecture](/wiki/Chiba_Prefecture "Chiba Prefecture") in Japan, [Jalisco](/wiki/Jalisco "Jalisco") in Mexico, [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang") in China, and [Nicaragua](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua").[[243]](#cite_note-iw2006-245) A [Mexican consulate](/wiki/Mexican_consulate "Mexican consulate") opened in Milwaukee on July 1, 2016.[[244]](#cite_note-246)

## Education

See also: [List of colleges and universities in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Wisconsin "List of colleges and universities in Wisconsin"), [List of high schools in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Wisconsin "List of high schools in Wisconsin"), and [List of school districts in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Wisconsin "List of school districts in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Bascom_Hall_Sunset.jpg/250px-Bascom_Hall_Sunset.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bascom_Hall_Sunset.jpg)

The [University of Wisconsin–Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison") is the flagship campus of the [University of Wisconsin System](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin_System "University of Wisconsin System").

Wisconsin, along with Michigan and Minnesota, was among the [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwestern "Midwestern") leaders in the emergent American state university movement following the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). By the start of the 20th century, education in the state advocated the "[Wisconsin Idea](/wiki/Wisconsin_Idea "Wisconsin Idea")", which emphasized service to the people of the state. The "Wisconsin Idea" exemplified the Progressive movement within colleges and universities at the time.[[245]](#cite_note-nye-247)

The [Wisconsin Department of Public Instruction](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Public_Instruction "Wisconsin Department of Public Instruction") is the [state education](/wiki/State_education_agency "State education agency") and public library management agency in the state.[[246]](#cite_note-248) The department is led by the [State Superintendent of Public Instruction](/wiki/Superintendent_of_Public_Instruction_of_Wisconsin "Superintendent of Public Instruction of Wisconsin"), a non-partisan, constitutional officer elected every four years in the spring primary, six months after the previous year's presidential election.

Public [post-secondary education](/wiki/Post-secondary_education "Post-secondary education") in Wisconsin consists of two organizations. The [University of Wisconsin System](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin_System "University of Wisconsin System") comprises the state's two tier 1 [research universities](/wiki/Research_universities "Research universities") as classified by the [Carnegie Foundation](/wiki/Carnegie_Foundation_for_the_Advancement_of_Teaching "Carnegie Foundation for the Advancement of Teaching"): the flagship [University of Wisconsin–Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison"), and the [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee").[[247]](#cite_note-249) The system has eleven other comprehensive universities and ten two-year branch campuses. It is one of the largest public higher education systems in the country, enrolling more than 160,000 [students](/wiki/University_student "University student") each year and employing approximately 41,000 faculty and staff statewide.[[248]](#cite_note-250) The 16-campus [Wisconsin Technical College System](/wiki/Wisconsin_Technical_College_System "Wisconsin Technical College System") awards two-year associate degrees, one- and two-year technical diplomas, and short-term technical diplomas and certificates. It also provides training and technical assistance to Wisconsin's business and industry community.

[Carroll University](/wiki/Carroll_University "Carroll University") was Wisconsin's first institution of higher education. It was chartered by the territorial legislature on January 31, 1846, and temporarily suspended operations during the Civil War.[[249]](#cite_note-251) [Beloit College](/wiki/Beloit_College "Beloit College") is the state's oldest continuously operated college, chartered on February 2, 1846.[[250]](#cite_note-252) [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University") is a tier 2 research university and Wisconsin's largest private university by enrollment.[[251]](#cite_note-253)[[252]](#cite_note-nces-254) It became the world's first coeducational Catholic university in 1909.[[253]](#cite_note-255) Other private colleges and universities include [Alverno College](/wiki/Alverno_College "Alverno College"), [Carthage College](/wiki/Carthage_College "Carthage College"), [Concordia University Wisconsin](/wiki/Concordia_University_Wisconsin "Concordia University Wisconsin"), [Edgewood University](/wiki/Edgewood_University "Edgewood University"), [Lakeland University](/wiki/Lakeland_University "Lakeland University"), [Lawrence University](/wiki/Lawrence_University "Lawrence University"), [Medical College of Wisconsin](/wiki/Medical_College_of_Wisconsin "Medical College of Wisconsin"), [Milwaukee School of Engineering](/wiki/Milwaukee_School_of_Engineering "Milwaukee School of Engineering"), [Ripon College](/wiki/Ripon_College_(Wisconsin) "Ripon College (Wisconsin)"), [St. Norbert College](/wiki/St._Norbert_College "St. Norbert College"), [Wisconsin Lutheran College](/wiki/Wisconsin_Lutheran_College "Wisconsin Lutheran College"), and [Viterbo University](/wiki/Viterbo_University "Viterbo University").

## Transportation

See also: [List of airports in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Wisconsin "List of airports in Wisconsin"), [List of state trunk highways in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_state_trunk_highways_in_Wisconsin "List of state trunk highways in Wisconsin"), [List of Wisconsin railroads](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_railroads "List of Wisconsin railroads"), and [List of lighthouses in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_lighthouses_in_Wisconsin "List of lighthouses in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Aerial_view_of_Wind_Point_Lighthouse%2C_Wisconsin%2C_US_julesvernex2.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_Wind_Point_Lighthouse%2C_Wisconsin%2C_US_julesvernex2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Wind_Point_Lighthouse,_Wisconsin,_US_julesvernex2.jpg)

[Wind Point Lighthouse](/wiki/Wind_Point_Lighthouse "Wind Point Lighthouse") on Lake Michigan

Wisconsin is served by eight commercial service airports, in addition to a number of [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airports. [Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mitchell_International_Airport "Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport") is the largest airport located in Wisconsin, [categorized](/wiki/FAA_airport_categories "FAA airport categories") as a medium-hub primary commercial service facility.[[254]](#cite_note-NPIAS_Airports-256) [Dane County Regional Airport](/wiki/Dane_County_Regional_Airport "Dane County Regional Airport") and [Appleton International Airport](/wiki/Appleton_International_Airport "Appleton International Airport") are classified as small-hub primary commercial airports that service the Madison and Fox Cities areas.

Wisconsin is served by multiple [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus "Intercity bus") operators, which provide service to 71 stops and 53 cities. The following carriers provide scheduled bus service: [Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway"), [Badger Bus](/wiki/Badger_Bus "Badger Bus"), [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), [Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails"), [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines"), [Lamers Bus Lines](/w/index.php?title=Lamers_Bus_Lines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lamers Bus Lines (page does not exist)"), [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)"), [Van Galder Bus Company](/wiki/Van_Galder_Bus_Company "Van Galder Bus Company"), and [Wisconsin Coach Lines](/wiki/Wisconsin_Coach_Lines "Wisconsin Coach Lines").[[255]](#cite_note-257)

Wisconsin is served by eight [Interstate Highways](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_Wisconsin "List of Interstate Highways in Wisconsin"), consisting of five [primary routes](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System#Primary_(one-_and_two-digit)_routes_(contiguous_U.S.) "Interstate Highway System") and three [auxiliary routes](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System#Auxiliary_(three-digit)_Interstates_(contiguous_U.S.) "Interstate Highway System"). The first part of this system was constructed in 1956, and its most recent expansion took place in 2015, with the addition of [I-41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41") to the system. Wisconsin's longest Interstate Highway is [I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 94 in Wisconsin"). There are also fourteen [United States Numbered Highways](/wiki/United_States_Numbered_Highways "United States Numbered Highways") in the state of Wisconsin, which were designated beginning in 1926 and ending in the mid-1930s. There are also several [business routes](/wiki/Business_route "Business route"), usually maintained by local governments. The [Wisconsin Department of Transportation](/wiki/Wisconsin_Department_of_Transportation "Wisconsin Department of Transportation") maintains [158 state trunk highways](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Trunk_Highway_System "Wisconsin State Trunk Highway System"), ranging from two-lane rural roads to limited-access freeways. These highways are paid for by the state's Transportation Fund, which is considered unique among state highway funds because it is kept entirely separate from the general fund, therefore, revenues received from transportation services are required to be used on transportation. The majority of state highway funding comes from gas taxes and vehicle registration fees.[[256]](#cite_note-258)

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") provides daily passenger rail service between Milwaukee and Chicago through the *[Hiawatha](/wiki/Hiawatha_(Amtrak_train) "Hiawatha (Amtrak train)")*. The *[Borealis](/wiki/Borealis_(train) "Borealis (train)")* provides daily service to Chicago and [Saint Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota"), and is supplemental to the long-distance cross-country *[Empire Builder](/wiki/Empire_Builder "Empire Builder")*, both with stops in several cities across Wisconsin.[[257]](#cite_note-259) Commuter rail provider [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra")'s [Union Pacific North (UP-N) line](/wiki/Union_Pacific_North_Line "Union Pacific North Line") has its northern terminus in [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha "Kenosha"), the only Metra line and station in the state of Wisconsin.[[258]](#cite_note-260) [The Hop](/wiki/The_Hop "The Hop"), a modern streetcar system in Milwaukee, began service in 2018. The 2.1 mile (3.4 km) initial line runs from [Milwaukee Intermodal Station](/wiki/Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station "Milwaukee Intermodal Station") to [Burns Commons](/wiki/Burns_Commons "Burns Commons").

## State symbols

See also: [List of Wisconsin state symbols](/wiki/List_of_Wisconsin_state_symbols "List of Wisconsin state symbols")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/What_are_you_looking_at%3F_%2848480462057%29.jpg/250px-What_are_you_looking_at%3F_%2848480462057%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:What_are_you_looking_at%3F_(48480462057).jpg)

The [American badger](/wiki/American_badger "American badger") is the [state animal](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mammals "List of U.S. state mammals") of Wisconsin.

Wisconsin is traditionally known as the "Badger State" due to its early history in [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining"). Many of the state's first settlers were drawn by the prospect of mining in southwest Wisconsin, a mineral-rich region which had been contested between [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and the U.S. Some of the miners lived burrowed within hillsides either due to lack of time or finances to build above-ground structures during the winter. Similar to the [American badger](/wiki/American_badger "American badger") using its claws to dig holes, the miners were nicknamed "badgers". The [University of Wisconsin–Madison](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Madison "University of Wisconsin–Madison") adopted the badger as a mascot in 1889 after the nickname; the badger was named Wisconsin's state animal in 1957.[[259]](#cite_note-261)

* [State nicknames](/wiki/State_nickname "State nickname"): *Badger State*, *America's Dairyland*
* [State motto](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_and_territory_mottos "List of U.S. state and territory mottos"): *Forward*
* [State song](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_songs "List of U.S. state songs"): "[On, Wisconsin!](/wiki/On,_Wisconsin! "On, Wisconsin!")"
* [State bird](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_birds "List of U.S. state birds"): [American robin](/wiki/American_robin "American robin")
* [State animal](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mammals "List of U.S. state mammals"): [American badger](/wiki/American_badger "American badger")
* [State domestic animal](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mammals "List of U.S. state mammals"): [dairy cow](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle")
* [State wildlife animal](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_mammals "List of U.S. state mammals"): [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer")
* [State fish](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_fish "List of U.S. state fish"): [muskellunge](/wiki/Muskellunge "Muskellunge")
* [State insect](/wiki/List_of_U.S._state_insects "List of U.S. state insects"): [Western honey bee](/wiki/Western_honey_bee "Western honey bee")
